This file contains the full text content of all pages on tennesseebankruptcy.org.
Part of the Open Bankruptcy Project (openbankruptcyproject.org).

## Bank Accounts and Bankruptcy in Tennessee
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/bank-account.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeMemphisNashvilleChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide›Bank Accounts

Bank Accounts and Bankruptcy in Tennessee

Understanding how bankruptcy interacts with your bank accounts in Tennessee can help you protect your money and avoid unexpected freezes or seizures.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Bank Account Levies and the Automatic Stay

Before bankruptcy, judgment creditors can obtain a court order to levy (freeze and seize) your bank account in Tennessee. The moment you file bankruptcy, the automatic stay prohibits all new levies.

New levies: Completely blocked by the stay

Pending levies: May need a motion to release frozen funds

Government debts: Tax levies are paused but not permanently blocked by the stay

Bank Setoff Rights

If you owe money to the same bank where your accounts are held (credit card, loan, line of credit), the bank has a right of setoff - meaning they can take money from your deposit account to cover what you owe, even without a court order.

How to Protect Yourself

Before filing: Open a new account at a bank or credit union where you have no debts

Move direct deposit: Switch your payroll direct deposit to the new bank

Keep records: Do not move large sums that could look like a fraudulent transfer

Timing: Coordinate with your attorney to minimize risk around the filing date

Do not drain accounts or make large transfers without attorney guidance. Transfers of property before filing can be scrutinized as potential fraudulent conveyances.

Protecting Bank Deposits in Tennessee

In Chapter 7, the trustee can claim non-exempt cash in your bank accounts on the date of filing. In Tennessee:

Homestead exemption: $5,000 (individual) / $7,500 (joint) (does not protect cash)

Vehicle exemption: $10,000 (does not protect cash)

Federal exemptions: If you elect federal exemptions, the wildcard exemption can protect cash deposits

Many Tennessee filers protect bank deposits by timing the filing when account balances are low (right after paying normal living expenses like rent, utilities, and food).

Tennessee exemptions guide →

Frequently Asked Questions

Can creditors freeze my bank account in Tennessee?

Yes. Before bankruptcy, a creditor with a judgment can levy (freeze and seize funds from) your bank account in Tennessee. After you file bankruptcy, the automatic stay immediately prohibits any new levies and may require release of frozen funds.

Should I open a new bank account before filing?

If you owe money to the same bank where you have checking or savings accounts, the bank may exercise its right of setoff - taking money from your account to cover the debt. Opening an account at a bank where you have no debts before filing is a common protective measure.

Will bankruptcy affect my bank account?

Filing bankruptcy does not close your bank accounts. However, a Chapter 7 trustee could claim non-exempt funds. In Tennessee, check which exemptions protect your cash and bank deposits.

Prepare Before You File

Check your discharge eligibility and learn how to protect your assets.

Discharge Screener
Tennessee Filing Checklist

## Can I Buy a Car While in Chapter 13 in Tennessee?
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/buy-car-chapter-13.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeMemphisNashvilleChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Buy Car in Ch. 13

Can I Buy a Car While in Chapter 13 in Tennessee?

Yes - but you need court permission first. Buying a car during Chapter 13 requires filing a motion, getting trustee approval, and showing the purchase fits your budget.

Quick answer: You can buy a car during Chapter 13 in Tennessee, but you must file a Motion to Incur New Debt and get court/trustee approval before signing anything. The court will evaluate whether the purchase is necessary and affordable within your plan.

The Process: Step by Step

1

Get Pre-Approved

Find a lender willing to work with Chapter 13 debtors. Get the terms in writing - vehicle, price, interest rate, monthly payment.

2

File the Motion

File a Motion to Incur New Debt with the Eastern, Middle, and Western Districts of Tennessee bankruptcy court. Include all purchase details and an updated budget.

3

Court Approval

The trustee reviews and may object. If no objection, the court typically grants the motion within 2-3 weeks. Do not sign anything until approved.

What the Court Wants to See

Necessity: Is the vehicle necessary for work, medical appointments, or family needs? Luxury vehicles are almost always denied.

Affordability: Can you make both the car payment AND your Chapter 13 plan payment? The court will review your current budget.

Reasonable terms: Interest rates above 20-25% may be questioned. The court wants to see you are not being exploited.

Vehicle value: A used car in the $5,000-$15,000 range is typical. Requests for new or expensive vehicles face heavier scrutiny.

Down payment source: The court may ask where the down payment is coming from. It should not come from plan payments or exempt funds earmarked for creditors.

Do NOT sign a purchase agreement, make a deposit, or take possession of a vehicle before the court grants your motion. Unauthorized debt during Chapter 13 can result in case dismissal.

Interest Rates and Lender Options

Borrowers in active Chapter 13 cases face limited lender options and higher interest rates. Typical options in Tennessee include:

Credit unions: Often the best rates for Chapter 13 borrowers. Some Tennessee credit unions specifically work with bankruptcy filers.

Subprime auto lenders: Companies like Capital One Auto Finance, Westlake Financial, and DriveTime specialize in lending to borrowers in bankruptcy. Rates range from 12-24%.

Buy-here-pay-here dealers: Available but typically the worst terms. Interest rates can exceed 25%, and vehicle quality is often poor.

Section 506 cramdown: If your current vehicle loan is more than 910 days old, you may be able to reduce the balance to the vehicle's value through your Chapter 13 plan rather than buying a new car.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I buy a car while in Chapter 13 in Tennessee?

Yes, but you need court approval. You must file a motion with the bankruptcy court requesting permission to incur new debt. The trustee and court will evaluate whether the purchase is necessary, whether you can afford it within your plan, and whether the terms are reasonable.

Do I need trustee approval to buy a car in Tennessee Chapter 13?

Yes. In Tennessee, as in all states, you must get trustee approval before taking on new debt during Chapter 13. The trustee will review the purchase price, interest rate, monthly payment, and whether it fits within your budget and plan obligations.

What interest rate can I expect when buying a car during Chapter 13?

Interest rates for borrowers in Chapter 13 are typically higher - often 15-25% from subprime lenders. Some credit unions and specialized lenders work with Chapter 13 debtors at better rates. The court may reject a purchase with an unreasonably high interest rate.

How do I file a motion to buy a car in Chapter 13?

You or your attorney file a Motion to Incur New Debt with the bankruptcy court. The motion should include the vehicle details, purchase price, interest rate, monthly payment, and an updated budget showing you can afford both the car payment and your plan payment.

Can I lease a car during Chapter 13 in Tennessee?

Leasing during Chapter 13 is treated the same as buying - you need court approval. Some courts view leases less favorably because they do not build equity. The total lease cost will be compared against purchasing.

What happens if I buy a car without court approval?

Taking on new debt without court approval is a violation of your Chapter 13 plan. The trustee may file a motion to dismiss your case or convert it to Chapter 7. The unauthorized debt could also be treated as fraudulent.

Related Topics

## Buying a House After Bankruptcy in Tennessee
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/buying-a-house.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeMemphisNashvilleChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide›Buying a House

Buying a House After Bankruptcy in Tennessee

Bankruptcy does not permanently prevent homeownership. Thousands of Americans buy homes after bankruptcy every year. Here are the waiting periods, requirements, and strategies for Tennessee residents.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Waiting Periods by Loan Type

Loan TypeAfter Chapter 7After Chapter 13

FHA Loan2 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval) or 2 years from discharge

VA Loan2 years from discharge1 year into plan (with lender approval)

USDA Loan3 years from discharge1 year into plan (with approval)

Conventional (Fannie/Freddie)4 years from discharge2 years from discharge or 4 years from dismissal

The waiting period starts from the discharge date, not the filing date. For Chapter 7, that is typically 3-4 months after filing. For Chapter 13, it is at the end of the 3-5 year plan.

Steps to Prepare for Homeownership

1

Rebuild Credit

Get a secured credit card immediately after discharge. Make small purchases and pay in full monthly. Consider a credit-builder loan. Target a 620+ score for FHA, 640+ for conventional.

2

Save for Down Payment

FHA requires 3.5% down with a 580+ score (10% with 500-579). Conventional requires 3-5%. VA and USDA offer zero-down options.

3

Stabilize Employment

Lenders want to see 2 years of stable employment. Consistent income documentation is critical. Self-employed borrowers may need additional tax returns.

Tennessee Homestead Exemption

When you do buy a home in Tennessee, the state homestead exemption protects $5,000 (individual) / $7,500 (joint) in home equity from future creditors. This means:

Your home equity up to $5,000 (individual) / $7,500 (joint) is protected from judgment creditors

If you ever need to file bankruptcy again, this equity is exempt from the Chapter 7 trustee

Tennessee allows choosing between state and federal exemptions, giving flexibility in protecting your home

Learn more about keeping your house in Tennessee bankruptcy →

Frequently Asked Questions

How long after bankruptcy can I buy a house in Tennessee?

After Chapter 7: 2-4 years depending on the loan type (FHA requires 2 years, conventional requires 4 years). After Chapter 13: 1-2 years after discharge (FHA may allow applications during an active plan with trustee approval). VA loans require 2 years after Chapter 7 and may allow applications during Chapter 13.

Can I get a mortgage during Chapter 13?

Some lenders and the FHA allow mortgage applications during an active Chapter 13 plan if you have made 12 consecutive on-time payments and receive court and trustee approval. This is difficult but not impossible.

Will bankruptcy destroy my credit score?

Bankruptcy causes an initial credit score drop of 100-200 points. However, many Tennessee filers see improvement within 12-18 months because the debt-to-income ratio improves and negative accounts stop accumulating late payments. Active rebuilding efforts accelerate recovery.

Start Planning Your Path to Homeownership

Check your discharge eligibility and learn about rebuilding credit after bankruptcy.

Discharge Screener
Rebuild Credit Guide

## Car Leases and Bankruptcy in Tennessee
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/car-lease.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeMemphisNashvilleChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Car Leases

Car Leases and Bankruptcy in Tennessee

You have two options: assume the lease (keep the car) or reject it (return the car). The 60-day clock starts when you file.

Quick answer: When you file bankruptcy in Tennessee, you must decide within 60 days whether to keep or surrender your leased vehicle. If you keep it, you must stay current. If you surrender it, remaining lease obligations become dischargeable unsecured debt.

Assume vs. Reject: Your Two Options

Assume (Keep the Car)

Continue making lease payments as normal

Cure any past-due payments

Lease continues as if bankruptcy never happened

Must not be a financial burden on your budget

Must decide within 60 days of filing

Good option if payments are affordable and you need the car

Reject (Return the Car)

Surrender the vehicle to the leasing company

Early termination fees become unsecured debt

Remaining lease payments become unsecured debt

Deficiency balance discharged in Chapter 7

Paid at reduced rate in Chapter 13 plan

Good option if payments are too high or car is not needed

The 60-Day Deadline

Under 11 U.S.C. section 365(d)(2), you have 60 days from the date of filing to assume or reject your car lease in Chapter 7. This deadline can be extended by the court for cause, but extensions are not automatic.

If you fail to act within 60 days, the lease is deemed rejected. This means you lose the vehicle and the remaining obligations become unsecured debt.

Mark your calendar: 60 days from your bankruptcy filing date is your deadline for the lease decision. Missing it means automatic rejection.

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 Lease Treatment

Chapter 7

60-day assume/reject deadline applies

Rejected lease: deficiency balance discharged

Assumed lease: continues normally

No option to modify lease terms

Best if you want a clean break from a bad lease

Chapter 13

Decision made as part of your plan

Can cure past-due payments through plan

Rejected lease: deficiency paid through plan at reduced rate

More flexibility on timing

Best if you are behind on payments but want to keep the car

Practical Considerations

Mileage and wear: If you are over your mileage limit or the car has excess wear, rejecting the lease in bankruptcy eliminates those penalties. They become dischargeable unsecured debt.

Gap insurance: If the car is worth less than the payoff, gap insurance may cover the difference. Bankruptcy rejection does not trigger gap insurance - it covers total loss/theft only.

New lease after bankruptcy: You can lease a new car after bankruptcy, but expect higher deposits and potentially higher rates. Some Tennessee dealers work with recent bankruptcy filers.

Automatic stay protection: While your case is pending, the leasing company cannot repossess the vehicle. If you plan to reject, you have time to arrange alternative transportation.

Frequently Asked Questions

What happens to my car lease if I file bankruptcy in Tennessee?

In both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13, you must decide whether to assume (keep) or reject (surrender) your car lease within 60 days of filing. If you assume, you keep the car and continue making payments. If you reject, you return the vehicle and any remaining balance is treated as unsecured debt.

What does it mean to assume a lease in bankruptcy?

Assuming a lease means you agree to keep the lease, stay current on all payments, and cure any past-due amounts. The lease continues as if bankruptcy was never filed. You must be current on payments and the vehicle must not be a financial burden.

What does it mean to reject a lease in bankruptcy?

Rejecting a lease means surrendering the vehicle to the lessor. Any early termination fees, remaining payments, or deficiency balance become unsecured debt that can be discharged in Chapter 7 or paid through a Chapter 13 plan at pennies on the dollar.

What is the 60-day deadline for lease decisions?

Under 11 U.S.C. section 365(d)(2), you must assume or reject an unexpired lease within 60 days of filing (for Chapter 7). If you do not act within 60 days, the lease is deemed rejected. In Chapter 13, you can assume or reject as part of your plan.

Can I get out of a bad car lease through bankruptcy in Tennessee?

Yes. Rejecting a car lease in bankruptcy is one of the cleanest ways to exit a bad lease. Early termination fees and remaining balance become unsecured debt. In Chapter 7, this debt is typically discharged entirely.

Will the leasing company repossess my car during bankruptcy?

No. The automatic stay prevents the leasing company from repossessing the vehicle once you file. However, you must make your decision within 60 days and stay current on payments if you want to keep the car.

Related Topics

Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Exemptions
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House
Credit After
Garnishment

## Chapter 13 Bankruptcy Eligibility in Tennessee
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/chapter-13-eligibility.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeMemphisNashvilleChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide›Chapter 13 Eligibility

Chapter 13 Bankruptcy Eligibility in Tennessee

Chapter 13 lets Tennessee residents keep their property while repaying debts over 3-5 years. Here are the eligibility requirements.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Core Eligibility Requirements

1. Regular Income

You must have regular income to fund a Chapter 13 repayment plan. Acceptable sources include:

Wages or salary

Self-employment income

Social Security benefits

Pension or retirement income

Rental income

Regular contributions from a spouse or household member

2. Debt Limits

Your total debts (secured plus unsecured) must not exceed $2,750,000. This combined limit was established by the Bankruptcy Threshold Adjustment and Technical Corrections Act of 2022.

3. Tax Filing

You must have filed all required federal and state tax returns for the four years before filing.

4. Credit Counseling

You must complete a credit counseling course from an approved provider within 180 days before filing.

Plan Length: 3 Years vs. 5 Years

Below Median Income

If your household income is at or below the Tennessee median, your plan can be as short as 36 months (3 years) and no longer than 60 months (5 years).

Above Median Income

If your household income exceeds the Tennessee median, your plan must be 60 months (5 years). This is called the applicable commitment period.

Prior Filing Bars for Chapter 13

Under 11 U.S.C. section 1328(f), you cannot receive a Chapter 13 discharge if:

You received a Chapter 7 discharge within the past 4 years

You received a Chapter 13 discharge within the past 2 years

These time bars only prevent a discharge - you can still file Chapter 13 to use the automatic stay and structured repayment even without eligibility for a discharge.

Check your eligibility with the 1328(f) Screener →

Chapter 13 Advantages in Tennessee

Keep your home: Catch up on mortgage arrears while protecting up to $5,000 (individual) / $7,500 (joint) in equity

Keep your car: Restructure auto loans (potential cramdown if older than 910 days) with $10,000 exemption protection

No means test barrier: Unlike Chapter 7, high earners can file Chapter 13

Co-debtor stay: Protects co-signers on consumer debts during the plan

Broader discharge: Some debts not dischargeable in Chapter 7 may be discharged in Chapter 13

Frequently Asked Questions

What are the debt limits for Chapter 13 in Tennessee?

As of 2024, the debt limit for Chapter 13 is $2,750,000 in combined secured and unsecured debts. This limit was updated by the Bankruptcy Threshold Adjustment and Technical Corrections Act. If your debts exceed this amount, you may need to file Chapter 11 instead.

Do I need regular income to file Chapter 13?

Yes. Chapter 13 requires regular income sufficient to fund a repayment plan. This can include wages, salary, commissions, self-employment income, Social Security, pension, rental income, or even consistent support from a spouse or domestic partner.

How long is a Chapter 13 plan in Tennessee?

If your income is below the Tennessee median for your household size, your plan can be 3-5 years. If your income is above the median, your plan must be 5 years. The plan cannot exceed 5 years (60 months).

Check Your Eligibility

See if prior filings affect your Chapter 13 discharge eligibility.

## Chapter 13 Payments in Tennessee
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/chapter-13-payments.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeMemphisNashvilleChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide›Chapter 13 Payments

Chapter 13 Payments in Tennessee

Your Chapter 13 plan payment is based on your income, expenses, and debts. Here is how it works in Tennessee and what to do if you cannot keep up.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

How Payments Are Calculated

Three tests determine your minimum Chapter 13 payment:

1. Disposable Income Test

Your monthly income minus reasonable living expenses (IRS standards and actual secured debt payments). All disposable income must go into the plan.

2. Liquidation Test (Best Interest of Creditors)

Unsecured creditors must receive at least what they would get if you filed Chapter 7. If you have significant non-exempt assets, this raises your payment.

3. Secured and Priority Debt Test

Your plan must pay all priority debts in full (taxes, child support arrears) and cure any secured debt arrears (mortgage, car loan). These are non-negotiable minimums.

Plan Length in Tennessee

Below Median Income

36-60 months. Your plan can be as short as 3 years but cannot exceed 5 years. Shorter plans mean higher monthly payments but less time in bankruptcy.

Above Median Income

60 months (required). If your income exceeds the Tennessee median for your household size, you must commit to a full 5-year plan.

What Gets Paid in a Chapter 13 Plan

Debt TypeTreatment

Mortgage arrearsPaid in full to cure default and keep home (homestead exemption: $5,000 (individual) / $7,500 (joint))

Car loanPaid through plan, possible cramdown if older than 910 days

Priority taxesPaid in full over plan length

Child support arrearsPaid in full

Unsecured debtPaid 0-100% depending on disposable income

Trustee feesTypically 5-10% of plan payments, built into payment amount

Missing Payments and Modification

If you fall behind on Chapter 13 payments:

Contact the trustee immediately - many will work with you on temporary hardship

File a plan modification if the change is permanent (job loss, pay cut, medical issues)

Hardship discharge may be available under section 1328(b) if you have paid the equivalent of what Chapter 7 creditors would have received

Convert to Chapter 7 if you no longer have sufficient income for any plan

Do not simply stop paying. Communicate with the trustee and your attorney to explore all options before your case is dismissed.

Frequently Asked Questions

How much will my Chapter 13 payment be in Tennessee?

Your monthly payment depends on your disposable income (income minus allowed expenses), the amount of secured and priority debts you must pay, and the liquidation test (unsecured creditors must receive at least what they would get in Chapter 7). Most Tennessee Chapter 13 payments range from a few hundred to a few thousand dollars per month.

What happens if I miss a Chapter 13 payment?

Missing payments can result in the trustee filing a motion to dismiss your case. If your case is dismissed, you lose the protection of the automatic stay and all the progress you have made. Contact the trustee immediately if you are going to be late - many allow temporary payment modifications.

Can I change my Chapter 13 payment amount?

Yes. You can file a modified plan with the W.D. Tenn., M.D. Tenn., and E.D. Tenn. court if your financial situation changes. Common reasons include job loss, pay cut, medical emergency, or increased expenses. The modified plan must still meet all legal requirements.

Explore Your Options

Tennessee Chapter 13 Guide
Discharge Screener

## Chapter 13 Bankruptcy in Tennessee - Payment Plans, Timeline
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/chapter-13.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQStatsResourcesOBP

Home›Chapter 13

Chapter 13 Bankruptcy in Tennessee

Chapter 13 lets you keep your property while repaying debts over 3-5 years. Understanding the process and your Tennessee exemptions is critical to success.

3-5 yrs

Plan Duration

$313

Filing Fee

$5,000 / $7,500

Homestead Exemption

What Is Chapter 13?

Chapter 13, sometimes called a "wage earner's plan," allows people with regular income to create a court-supervised repayment plan lasting 3 to 5 years. You make monthly payments to a bankruptcy trustee, who distributes the money to your creditors according to the plan.

Unlike Chapter 7, Chapter 13 does not require liquidating assets. You keep all your property. At the end of the plan, remaining eligible debts are discharged.

Chapter 13 is particularly useful for:

Saving a home from foreclosure by catching up on missed mortgage payments

Keeping a car while catching up on auto loan payments

Paying tax debts or other priority debts over time

Protecting non-exempt property that would be lost in Chapter 7

Filers who do not qualify for Chapter 7 under the means test

How a Chapter 13 Plan Works

The Monthly Payment

Your monthly payment is calculated based on your disposable income - the difference between your income and reasonable living expenses. The payment must cover:

Priority debts in full: Recent taxes, child support arrears, attorney fees

Secured debt arrears: Mortgage arrears, car loan arrears

Trustee fees: Typically around 10% of plan payments

Unsecured creditors: Whatever remains goes to credit cards, medical bills - often pennies on the dollar

Plan Duration: 3 or 5 Years?

If your income is below the Tennessee median, you may be able to complete a 3-year plan. If your income is above the median, your plan must be 5 years. The court will not approve a plan longer than 5 years.

What Happens at the End?

After completing all plan payments, the court enters a discharge under 11 U.S.C. section 1328. This eliminates remaining eligible unsecured debts. You keep your home, car, and other property.

If you received a prior discharge, time bars under section 1328(f) may prevent you from receiving another discharge.

Chapter 13 Timeline

Before filing: Complete credit counseling ($15-25). Gather income documentation, tax returns, and debt records.

Day 1 - Filing: Petition filed. Automatic stay takes effect. Foreclosures, repossessions, and garnishments stop immediately.

Day 14: Proposed plan filed (if not filed with the petition).

Day 30: First plan payment due to the trustee.

Day 30-45: 341 meeting of creditors. The trustee reviews your plan and finances.

Day 45-120: Confirmation hearing. The court decides whether to approve your plan.

Years 1-5: Monthly payments to the trustee. Report income changes. File tax returns on time.

Plan completion: Complete debtor education course. Court enters discharge.

Frequently Asked Questions

How long is a Chapter 13 repayment plan?

Chapter 13 plans last 3 to 5 years. If your income is below the Tennessee median, you may qualify for a 3-year plan. If above the median, your plan must be 5 years. During this time, you make monthly payments to a trustee who distributes the money to your creditors.

Can I keep my house in Chapter 13?

Yes. One of the main benefits of Chapter 13 is the ability to keep your home and catch up on missed mortgage payments through your repayment plan. The automatic stay stops foreclosure proceedings immediately upon filing. Tennessee's homestead exemption of $5,000 / $7,500 also applies.

What happens if my Chapter 13 is dismissed?

If your Chapter 13 case is dismissed, the automatic stay ends and creditors can resume collection activity. You lose the protection of the bankruptcy court. Any payments you made through the plan were distributed to creditors, but you do not receive a discharge of your remaining debts. Learn more.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

## Chapter 7 Bankruptcy Eligibility in Tennessee
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/chapter-7-eligibility.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeMemphisNashvilleChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide›Chapter 7 Eligibility

Chapter 7 Bankruptcy Eligibility in Tennessee

Chapter 7 bankruptcy can eliminate most unsecured debt in 3-4 months. Here is how to determine whether you qualify to file in Tennessee.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

The Means Test

The primary eligibility requirement for Chapter 7 is the means test under 11 U.S.C. section 707(b). It has two parts:

Step 1: Income Comparison

Compare your current monthly income (averaged over the 6 months before filing) to the Tennessee median income for your household size. If your income is at or below the median, you automatically pass.

Step 2: Expense Deductions (If Above Median)

If your income exceeds the median, you deduct allowed expenses (IRS standards plus actual secured debt payments) from your income. If the remaining disposable income is below the threshold, you still qualify for Chapter 7.

Tip: Use the means test calculator to check your eligibility before paying for a consultation.

Prior Filing Bars

Under 11 U.S.C. section 727(a)(8) and (a)(9), you cannot receive a Chapter 7 discharge if:

You received a Chapter 7 discharge within the past 8 years

You received a Chapter 13 discharge within the past 6 years (unless you paid 100% of unsecured claims or at least 70% in a good-faith plan)

Use the 1328(f) Screener to check whether prior filings affect your eligibility.

Other Eligibility Requirements

Credit counseling: Must complete an approved course within 180 days before filing

No recent dismissals for cause: If a case was dismissed in the past 180 days for violating a court order or failure to appear, you cannot file immediately

Domicile requirement: You file in the W.D. Tenn., M.D. Tenn., and E.D. Tenn. if you have lived in Tennessee for the greater portion of the past 180 days

No fraud: Fraudulent transfers or concealment of assets can bar discharge under section 727(a)(2)

Tax returns: You must provide your most recent tax return to the trustee at least 7 days before the 341 meeting

Tennessee Exemption Snapshot

If you qualify for Chapter 7, your exempt property is protected from liquidation:

Homestead: $5,000 (individual) / $7,500 (joint)

Vehicle: $10,000

Federal exemptions: Tennessee permits choosing federal exemptions as an alternative

Complete Tennessee exemptions list →

Frequently Asked Questions

What are the income limits for Chapter 7 in Tennessee?

To qualify for Chapter 7 in Tennessee, your household income must be at or below the state median income for your household size. If above median, you must pass the means test, which deducts allowed expenses from your income. If the result shows insufficient disposable income, you still qualify. Current Tennessee median income figures are updated twice yearly.

What disqualifies you from filing Chapter 7?

You may be disqualified from Chapter 7 if: you received a Chapter 7 discharge within the past 8 years, a Chapter 13 discharge within the past 6 years, your income is too high to pass the means test, you committed bankruptcy fraud, or you had a case dismissed within 180 days for specific reasons.

Do I have to take a class before filing Chapter 7 in Tennessee?

Yes. You must complete a credit counseling course from an approved provider within 180 days before filing. After filing, you must also complete a financial management course before receiving your discharge. Both courses can be done online and typically cost $25-$50 each.

Check Your Eligibility Now

Our free screener checks prior filings and time bars instantly.

Discharge Screener

## Chapter 7 Bankruptcy in Tennessee - Eligibility, Exemptions, Timeline
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/chapter-7.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQStatsResourcesOBP

Home›Chapter 7

Chapter 7 Bankruptcy in Tennessee

Chapter 7 is the fastest path to debt relief. Most unsecured debts are eliminated in 3-4 months. Here is what Tennessee residents need to know.

What Is Chapter 7?

Chapter 7, sometimes called "liquidation bankruptcy" or a "fresh start," eliminates most unsecured debts. A court-appointed trustee reviews your assets. If you have non-exempt property, the trustee may sell it to pay creditors - but most Chapter 7 cases are "no-asset" cases where the filer keeps everything.

Tennessee allows filers to choose between state and federal exemptions.

What Debts Does Chapter 7 Eliminate?

Dischargeable (eliminated)

Credit card debt

Medical bills

Personal loans and payday loans

Old utility bills

Deficiency balances from repossessions

Some older tax debts

Civil judgments (in most cases)

Not dischargeable

Student loans (except in rare hardship cases)

Recent income taxes (generally last 3 years)

Child support and alimony

Court-ordered restitution and fines

Debts from fraud or willful injury

Certain debts under 11 U.S.C. section 523(a)

The Means Test in Tennessee

To file Chapter 7, you must pass the means test. This compares your household income to the Tennessee median:

Below median income: You automatically qualify for Chapter 7

Above median income: A detailed calculation of your disposable income determines eligibility

For a single filer in Tennessee, the current median is approximately $48,000/year. These figures increase with household size.

Tennessee Exemptions in Chapter 7

Exemptions determine what property you keep.

PropertyTennessee Exemption

Homestead$5,000 / $7,500

VehicleNo specific vehicle exemption under state law (federal: $4,450)

Household goods$4,000 total (state)

Wildcard$10,000 (state wildcard); federal: $1,475 + up to $13,950 unused homestead

Tools of trade$1,900

Retirement (IRA/401k)Fully exempt

Social Security benefitsFully exempt

Full exemption details →

Chapter 7 Timeline

Weeks 1-2: Preparation - Gather documents (tax returns, pay stubs, bank statements). Complete credit counseling course ($15-25).

Day 1: Filing - Petition filed with the court. Automatic stay takes effect immediately - creditors must stop all collection activity.

Day 30-45: 341 Meeting - Brief meeting of creditors. The trustee asks questions under oath about your finances. Most meetings last 5-10 minutes.

Day 30-60: Debtor education - Complete the second required financial education course ($10-25).

Day 90-120: Discharge - The court enters your discharge order, eliminating eligible debts.

Frequently Asked Questions

What debts does Chapter 7 eliminate?

Chapter 7 discharges most unsecured debts including credit cards, medical bills, personal loans, and old utility bills. It does not discharge student loans (in most cases), recent taxes, child support, alimony, or debts incurred through fraud. See section 523(a) for the full list.

What is the means test for Chapter 7 in Tennessee?

The means test compares your household income to the Tennessee median. If your income is below the median for your household size, you automatically qualify. If above, a more detailed calculation determines whether you have enough disposable income to fund a Chapter 13 plan instead. For a single filer in Tennessee, the current median is approximately $48,000/year.

How long does Chapter 7 take?

From filing to discharge, Chapter 7 typically takes 3-4 months. The 341 meeting of creditors occurs about 30 days after filing, and the discharge is usually entered about 60 days after that.

What property can I keep in Chapter 7 in Tennessee?

Tennessee exemptions protect: homestead equity up to $5,000 / $7,500, vehicle equity up to No specific vehicle exemption under state law (federal: $4,450), household goods up to $4,000 total (state), and retirement accounts are fully exempt. Full exemption details.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

## Chapter 13 Bankruptcy in Chattanooga - Tennessee
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/chattanooga/chapter-13.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsFAQMemphisNashvilleKnoxvilleChattanoogaOBP

Home›Chattanooga›Chapter 13

Chapter 13 Bankruptcy in Chattanooga

Chapter 13 lets you keep property while repaying debts over 3-5 years.

How Chapter 13 Works

Propose a repayment plan over 3-5 years. Keep your home and car while catching up on arrears.

Save your home from foreclosure

Keep your car and cure defaults

Stop wage garnishment via automatic stay

Filing fee: $313

Non-Judicial Foreclosure

Tennessee is a non-judicial foreclosure state. Chapter 13's automatic stay is your strongest defense against losing your home.

How Chapter 13 Works for Chattanooga Filers

Chapter 13 is a court-supervised repayment plan, typically running three to five years. It is the right tool when you have regular income, want to catch up on a mortgage arrearage, protect a non-exempt asset, or failed the Chapter 7 means test. In Chattanooga, Chapter 13 plans are administered by the standing trustee for your division, who collects your monthly payment and distributes it to creditors according to the confirmed plan.

The Chapter 13 process starts the same way as Chapter 7: credit counseling, petition, schedules, and the automatic stay. The key differences begin at filing. Along with your schedules, you file a proposed plan showing how much you will pay the trustee each month, for how many months, and how that money will be distributed. Your first plan payment is due within 30 days of filing - even before the plan is confirmed by the judge - and missing it is the single most common reason Chapter 13 cases are dismissed early.

Your plan payment is calculated by taking your monthly disposable income (from Form 122C) and applying a few rules. Above-median debtors must commit all disposable income for 60 months. Below-median debtors must commit all disposable income for 36 months. Secured debts being cured in the plan - a mortgage arrearage, a car loan being crammed down - are added on top. Priority debts like recent taxes and child support arrearage must be paid in full through the plan. General unsecured creditors get whatever is left after all of that.

When Chapter 13 Makes Sense

Mortgage arrearage: Chapter 13 lets you cure a foreclosure arrearage over up to 60 months while making current payments outside the plan.

Underwater car loans over 910 days old: You can "cram down" the loan to the car's actual value and pay the rest as unsecured.

Non-dischargeable tax debt: Priority taxes can be paid over 60 months with the automatic stay in place, stopping IRS collection.

Above-median income: If you failed the Chapter 7 means test, Chapter 13 is the fallback and always available.

Keeping non-exempt assets: If you have equity above your exemption limits, Chapter 13 lets you keep the asset in exchange for paying the equity value to unsecured creditors through the plan.

About half of all Chapter 13 cases nationally fail to reach discharge. The most common reasons are missed plan payments and post-filing income disruptions. Before filing, make sure the plan payment is actually affordable given your real monthly budget - not an optimistic budget built to squeeze past the confirmation hearing.

Frequently Asked Questions

How long does a Chattanooga Chapter 13 plan last?

Three years if you are below the state median income, five years if you are above. The judge cannot confirm a plan shorter than three years unless it pays 100 percent of unsecured claims.

Can I pay off my Chattanooga Chapter 13 plan early?

Only by paying 100 percent of unsecured claims. If you try to pay off a plan early with less than 100 percent to unsecureds, the trustee and unsecured creditors can object - and courts generally sustain those objections because the Bankruptcy Code requires committing all projected disposable income for the full commitment period.

What happens if I miss a Chapter 13 payment in Chattanooga?

The trustee will file a motion to dismiss after one or two missed payments. You have a chance to cure the default by catching up, modifying the plan to add the missed amount to future payments, or converting to Chapter 7. Do not ignore a motion to dismiss - dismissal without a discharge means all your creditors come back.

Related resources from the Bankruptcy Transparency Network: means test, Chapter 7 vs. 13, 1328(f) prior-discharge screener, 341 meeting, automatic stay, how to file bankruptcy, student loans, Open Bankruptcy Project.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

Free Discharge Screener

## Chapter 7 Bankruptcy in Chattanooga - Tennessee
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/chattanooga/chapter-7.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsFAQMemphisNashvilleKnoxvilleChattanoogaOBP

Home›Chattanooga›Chapter 7

Chapter 7 Bankruptcy in Chattanooga

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts in 3-4 months. Here is what Chattanooga residents need to know.

What Is Chapter 7?

Chapter 7 eliminates credit cards, medical bills, and personal loans. Most Chattanooga filers keep all property within exemption limits.

Means Test

HouseholdAnnual Median

1$53,697

2$67,854

3$77,379

4$94,416

Timeline

1Credit counseling

2File at E.D. Tenn. Chattanooga, 900 Georgia Ave, Suite 109, Chattanooga, TN 37402

3341 meeting (~30 days)

4Financial management course

5Discharge (3-4 months)

Your Chapter 7 Case in Chattanooga - What to Expect

Chapter 7 is the fastest path out of unmanageable debt. In Chattanooga, most Chapter 7 cases close 90 to 120 days from the filing date, and the vast majority are 'no asset' cases where the debtor keeps everything they own. The automatic stay starts the moment you file, which means creditors cannot call, sue, garnish, or repossess while your case is pending. Here is what the process actually looks like for Chattanooga filers.

Before you file, you complete a pre-filing credit counseling course from an approved provider - usually online, about 60 minutes, under $25. You also gather your documents: the last two years of tax returns, the last six months of pay stubs, three months of bank statements, titles to your vehicles, your mortgage statement if you own a home, and a list of every creditor. Your petition, schedules, and statement of financial affairs are then filed electronically with the bankruptcy court. Filing opens a new case number and triggers the automatic stay.

About 20 to 40 days after filing, you attend the section 341 meeting of creditors. In Tennessee, most 341 meetings are now conducted by Zoom or phone rather than in person. You are placed under oath and questioned by the Chapter 7 trustee about your schedules. Typical questions: Did you list all your assets? Have you transferred anything valuable in the last two years? Do you expect a tax refund or inheritance? The meeting usually lasts five to ten minutes. Creditors rarely attend.

After the 341, the trustee investigates whether any of your assets can be sold for the benefit of creditors. In the great majority of Chattanooga Chapter 7 cases, the answer is no - your exempt property is protected, and the trustee files a "no distribution" report. You complete a second class (a post-filing financial management course) and wait. Roughly 60 days after the 341 meeting, the court issues your discharge order, wiping out the dischargeable debts and giving you a fresh start.

What Chapter 7 Does Not Wipe Out

Student loans in most cases (requires adversary proceeding for hardship discharge)

Recent tax debts (generally anything less than three years old)

Child support and alimony

Criminal fines and restitution

Debts obtained by fraud or false pretenses

Debts not listed on your schedules

Frequently Asked Questions

How long does a Chattanooga Chapter 7 take from filing to discharge?

Most cases discharge 90 to 120 days after filing. The minimum statutory period is 60 days after the 341 meeting. Cases with asset sales or objections take longer - sometimes a year or more.

Can I keep my car in a Chattanooga Chapter 7 case?

Usually yes, if you are current on the loan and your equity is within your exemption limits. You can either 'reaffirm' the debt (sign a new agreement to keep paying), 'redeem' it (pay the current value in one lump sum), or let it go. Most filers reaffirm.

How much does a Chapter 7 attorney cost in Chattanooga?

Attorney fees vary, but the typical range for a straightforward consumer Chapter 7 in most districts runs from about $1,000 to $2,500 plus the $338 court filing fee. Be careful with firms advertising '$0 down' Chapter 7 - upfront attorney fees in Chapter 7 are discharged if unpaid at filing, so 'no money down' is usually a Chapter 13 pitch.

Related resources from the Bankruptcy Transparency Network: means test, Chapter 7 vs. 13, 1328(f) prior-discharge screener, 341 meeting, automatic stay, how to file bankruptcy, student loans, Open Bankruptcy Project.

## Chattanooga Bankruptcy Exemptions - State vs Federal - Tennessee
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/chattanooga/exemptions.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsFAQMemphisNashvilleKnoxvilleChattanoogaOBP

Home›Chattanooga›Exemptions

Chattanooga Bankruptcy Exemptions - State vs Federal

Tennessee lets you choose state or federal exemptions.

Exemption Comparison

PropertyTennessee ExemptionFederal Alternative

Homestead$5,000 individual / $7,500 family$27,900

Vehicle$10,000 (wildcard)$4,450

Household goods$4,000 total$700 per item / $14,875 total

Wildcard$10,000$1,475 + up to $13,950 unused homestead

Retirement (IRA/401k)Fully exemptFully exempt

Social SecurityFully exemptFully exempt

Homeowners: federal ($27,900) usually wins. Renters: state's $10,000 wildcard may protect more personal property.

Tennessee Exemptions Explained for Chattanooga Filers

Exemptions are the rules that decide what you keep when you file bankruptcy. Everything you own becomes part of the bankruptcy estate the moment you file, and exemptions are the legal carve-outs that pull property back out of the estate and into your hands. For Chattanooga residents, the exemption question has three parts: which set of exemptions you can use, how much each exemption protects, and what happens to property that exceeds the limits.

State exemption law governs in most Tennessee bankruptcy cases. The federal exemptions in 11 U.S.C. section 522(d) are available only in "opt-in" states; in opt-out states, filers must use the state set. Check your state's current opt-in status before relying on federal numbers - some states allow a choice, others do not. In either case, you cannot mix and match: you pick one entire set of exemptions, not the best items from each.

The biggest exemption for most filers is the homestead - the equity in your primary residence you can protect from creditors. Homestead amounts vary wildly by state, from a few thousand dollars in some states to unlimited in others (Florida, Texas, Kansas, Iowa, Oklahoma, South Dakota). Domicile rules matter here: if you moved to your current state within the last 730 days, you may have to use the exemptions of your previous state under 11 U.S.C. section 522(b)(3)(A). Plan the filing timing accordingly.

Common Exemption Categories

Homestead: Equity in your primary residence.

Motor vehicle: Equity in one or more vehicles, usually a few thousand dollars per vehicle.

Personal property: Household goods, clothing, appliances, up to a cap.

Tools of the trade: Equipment you need to earn a living.

Retirement accounts: 401(k), pension, and IRA balances are almost always fully protected.

Wildcard: A dollar amount you can apply to any property, including cash.

Life insurance cash value: Usually protected up to a cap.

Public benefits: Social Security, unemployment, workers compensation, veterans benefits.

If you own property that exceeds your exemption limit - say, a paid-off car worth $12,000 when your exemption protects only $4,000 - the Chapter 7 trustee has the right to sell it, pay you the exempt amount, and distribute the rest to your creditors. In practice, trustees often negotiate with the debtor to buy the non-exempt portion rather than actually liquidate. Alternatively, you can file Chapter 13 and pay the non-exempt value to unsecureds through your plan instead of surrendering the asset.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I use the federal bankruptcy exemptions instead of Tennessee?

It depends on whether Tennessee has opted out of the federal exemption scheme. Most states have opted out, meaning you must use the state exemptions. A handful of states (and DC) allow filers to choose. Confirm current opt-in status before you file - it changes how you fill out Schedule C.

What if I moved to this state less than two years ago?

Under 11 U.S.C. section 522(b)(3)(A), if you have not been domiciled in your current state for the 730 days before filing, you use the exemptions of the state where you lived the greater part of the 180 days before that 730-day window. If that earlier state only allows its exemptions for residents, you may be forced into the federal set as a fallback.

Are retirement accounts safe in bankruptcy?

Yes, almost always. ERISA-qualified plans (401(k), 403(b), pension) are excluded from the estate entirely. Traditional and Roth IRAs are exempt up to a federal cap that is adjusted periodically - currently over $1.5 million in aggregate. Inherited IRAs are treated differently and may not receive the same protection under Clark v. Rameker (2014).

Related resources from the Bankruptcy Transparency Network: means test, Chapter 7 vs. 13, 1328(f) prior-discharge screener, 341 meeting, automatic stay, how to file bankruptcy, student loans, Open Bankruptcy Project.

## Chattanooga Bankruptcy FAQ - Tennessee
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/chattanooga/faq.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsFAQMemphisNashvilleKnoxvilleChattanoogaOBP

Home›Chattanooga›FAQ

Chattanooga Bankruptcy FAQ

Answers to common questions about filing bankruptcy in Chattanooga.

Where do I file?

E.D. Tenn. Chattanooga Division, 900 Georgia Ave, Suite 109, Chattanooga, TN 37402. Phone: (423) 752-5163.

How much does bankruptcy cost?

Chapter 7: $1,238-$2,188. Chapter 13: $2,838-$4,363.

State or federal exemptions?

Your choice. Federal homestead ($27,900) is higher. Compare.

How long does bankruptcy take?

Chapter 7: 3-4 months. Chapter 13: 3-5 years.

What is the 341 meeting?

Brief trustee hearing ~30 days after filing. Learn more.

Can I file again?

Yes, with timing rules. Check eligibility.

## Bankruptcy in Chattanooga, Tennessee - Complete Guide
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/chattanooga/

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsFAQMemphisNashvilleKnoxvilleChattanoogaOBP

Bankruptcy in Chattanooga, Tennessee

Chattanooga residents file in the Eastern District of Tennessee, Chattanooga Division. This free guide covers Chapter 7, Chapter 13, Tennessee exemptions, costs, and what to expect.

Read the FAQ
Check Your Eligibility

$5,000 / $7,500

TN Homestead Exemption

$27,900

Federal Homestead Option

$338

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$313

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13

Chapter 7 - Fresh Start

Eliminates most unsecured debts in 3-4 months. Must pass the means test. Filing fee: $338.
Chapter 7 guide →

Chapter 13 - Payment Plan

Keep property, repay over 3-5 years. No means test. Filing fee: $313.
Chapter 13 guide →

Chattanooga Courthouse

Eastern District of Tennessee - Chattanooga Division

Courthouse: 900 Georgia Ave, Suite 109, Chattanooga, TN 37402

Phone: (423) 752-5163 | tneb.uscourts.gov

Part of the Eastern District of Tennessee. Chattanooga has its own division separate from the Knoxville division.

Tennessee Exemptions

Choose state or federal exemptions. Federal homestead ($27,900) is typically better for homeowners.

PropertyTennessee ExemptionFederal Alternative

Homestead$5,000 individual / $7,500 family$27,900

Vehicle$10,000 (wildcard)$4,450

Household goods$4,000 total$700 per item / $14,875 total

Wildcard$10,000$1,475 + up to $13,950 unused homestead

Retirement (IRA/401k)Fully exemptFully exempt

Social SecurityFully exemptFully exempt

Full exemption details →

Free Legal Resources

Legal Aid of East Tennessee - Chattanooga Office

Phone: (423) 756-4013 | Free legal assistance for low-income Chattanooga residents.

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does bankruptcy cost in Chattanooga?

Chapter 7: $1,238-$2,188 total. Chapter 13: $2,838-$4,363.

Where do I file?

E.D. Tenn. Chattanooga Division, 900 Georgia Ave, Suite 109, Chattanooga, TN 37402.

State or federal exemptions?

Your choice. Federal homestead is $27,900 vs state $5,000/$7,500. Compare.

See all FAQ →

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

## Converting Bankruptcy Chapters in Tennessee
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/converting-chapters.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeMemphisNashvilleChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide›Converting Chapters

Converting Bankruptcy Chapters in Tennessee

If your financial situation changes after filing, Tennessee debtors can convert between bankruptcy chapters. Understanding your conversion rights can help you get the best outcome.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Conversion Options

Chapter 7 to Chapter 13

One-time right of conversion under section 706(a)

Common when you have assets at risk of liquidation

Useful when income increases or you want to save property

Must be able to propose a feasible repayment plan

No additional filing fee

Chapter 13 to Chapter 7

Right to convert at any time under section 1307(a)

Not available if case was originally Chapter 7 and converted to 13

Common when income drops and plan payments become unaffordable

Must still pass the means test if required

No additional filing fee

Common Reasons for Conversion in Tennessee

Job loss or income reduction - Chapter 13 plan payments become unaffordable, convert to Chapter 7

Income increase - No longer pass the means test for Chapter 7, convert to Chapter 13

Property at risk - Chapter 7 trustee identifies non-exempt property, convert to Chapter 13 to protect it

Mortgage arrears - Need Chapter 13's structured repayment to catch up on missed mortgage payments and keep your home (homestead exemption: $5,000 (individual) / $7,500 (joint))

Plan failure - Unable to complete Chapter 13 plan, convert to Chapter 7 for immediate discharge

What Happens When You Convert

File a motion or notice of conversion with the W.D. Tenn., M.D. Tenn., and E.D. Tenn. bankruptcy court

New schedules may be required - particularly if significant time has passed or circumstances have changed

New trustee is assigned - Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 trustees are different

The automatic stay continues - no gap in protection

A new 341 meeting may be scheduled - the new trustee needs to examine you

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I convert my bankruptcy case to a different chapter?

Yes. Under 11 U.S.C. section 706 and section 1307, you generally have the right to convert between chapters. Chapter 7 debtors can convert to Chapter 13 once as a matter of right. Chapter 13 debtors can convert to Chapter 7 at any time if they have not previously converted from Chapter 7.

Does converting restart my case?

Converting does not start a completely new case. Your case number stays the same, and the automatic stay remains in effect. However, new schedules and forms may need to be filed, and certain deadlines reset.

Are there fees to convert?

There is no additional filing fee to convert from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7. Converting from Chapter 7 to Chapter 13 requires paying the difference in filing fees ($313 minus $338, so no additional fee). Attorney fees may apply for the additional work.

Not Sure Which Chapter Is Right?

Compare the two main consumer bankruptcy chapters side by side.

Ch. 7 vs 13 Guide

## How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Tennessee? - Filing Fees, Attorney Fees
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/cost.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQStatsResourcesOBP

Home›Cost

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Tennessee?

Bankruptcy has real costs, but they are often far less than the debt you are trying to eliminate. Here is a transparent breakdown of every cost you should expect.

$338

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$313

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

$25-50

Credit Counseling (2 courses)

Chapter 7 Cost Breakdown

Cost ItemAmountNotes

Court filing fee$338Can pay in installments; fee waiver available

Attorney fees$900 to $1,800Typical Tennessee range; usually paid upfront

Pre-filing credit counseling$15 - $25Required within 180 days before filing

Post-filing debtor education$10 - $25Required before discharge

Credit report$0 - $30Free from annualcreditreport.com

Fee Waiver (Chapter 7 Only)

If your household income is below 150% of the federal poverty guidelines, you can apply to have the $338 filing fee waived entirely. You file the fee waiver application (Official Form 103B) with your petition.

Chapter 13 Cost Breakdown

Cost ItemAmountNotes

Court filing fee$313Can be paid through your plan

Attorney fees$2,500 to $4,000Typical Tennessee range; mostly paid through plan

Pre-filing credit counseling$15 - $25Required within 180 days before filing

Post-filing debtor education$10 - $25Required before discharge

The Chapter 13 Payment Advantage

Attorney fees and the filing fee can be included in your repayment plan. You typically need only a small upfront payment (often $0-$500), with the rest paid through your monthly plan payments over 3-5 years.

Ways to Reduce Costs

Free and Low-Cost Options

Legal Aid Society of Middle Tennessee - Free legal services for low-income residents of the Nashville area.

Memphis Area Legal Services - Free legal help for low-income Memphis residents.

Legal Aid of East Tennessee - Free legal services for eastern Tennessee.

Fee waiver - eliminates the $338 Chapter 7 filing fee for income below 150% of poverty

Installment payments - pay the filing fee in up to 4 installments over 120 days

Pro se filing - filing without an attorney is possible but not recommended for most filers

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does Chapter 7 cost in Tennessee?

The Chapter 7 filing fee is $338. Attorney fees in Tennessee typically range from $900 to $1,800. Credit counseling courses cost about $25-50 total (two required). Fee waivers are available if your income is below 150% of the federal poverty guidelines. National cost comparison.

How much does Chapter 13 cost in Tennessee?

The Chapter 13 filing fee is $313. Attorney fees typically range from $2,500 to $4,000 in Tennessee. The filing fee and attorney fees can be paid through your repayment plan, so you usually do not need to pay everything upfront.

Can I file bankruptcy for free?

The court filing fee can be waived in Chapter 7 if your income is below 150% of the federal poverty guidelines. Legal aid organizations in Tennessee may provide free or reduced-cost legal representation for qualifying individuals.

Do I have to pay an attorney upfront?

For Chapter 7, most attorneys require full payment before filing. For Chapter 13, attorney fees are typically included in your repayment plan, so you may only need a small upfront payment.

## Credit Card Debt and Bankruptcy in Tennessee
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/credit-card-debt.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeMemphisNashvilleChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide›Credit Card Debt

Credit Card Debt and Bankruptcy in Tennessee

Credit card debt is one of the most common reasons Tennessee residents file for bankruptcy. In most cases, credit card balances can be completely eliminated through Chapter 7 or significantly reduced through Chapter 13.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

How Each Chapter Handles Credit Card Debt

Chapter 7

Credit card debt is eliminated entirely in Chapter 7. The discharge typically comes in 3-4 months. You must pass the means test to qualify. Any non-exempt assets could be liquidated, but most Tennessee filers keep all their property under the state's exemptions.

Chapter 13

Credit card debt is included in your 3-5 year repayment plan. You pay a percentage of the total based on your disposable income. Many filers pay only 10-30 cents on the dollar. The remaining balance is discharged at the end of the plan.

Recent Charges: The 90-Day and 70-Day Rules

Under 11 U.S.C. section 523(a)(2)(C), certain recent credit card activity is presumed to be non-dischargeable:

Luxury goods/services over $800 charged within 90 days before filing

Cash advances over $1,100 within 70 days before filing

These are rebuttable presumptions. The credit card company must file an adversary proceeding to challenge discharge, and you can present evidence that the charges were made with the intent and ability to repay.

Best practice: Stop using credit cards well before filing. Using cards when you know you will file bankruptcy can be considered fraud.

Credit Card Lawsuits and Judgments in Tennessee

If a credit card company sues you in Tennessee and obtains a judgment, they can:

Garnish your wages

Levy your bank account

Place a lien on your property

Filing bankruptcy stops all of these through the automatic stay. Pre-existing judgment liens on personal property can often be avoided (removed) under 11 U.S.C. section 522(f) if they impair your Tennessee exemptions.

Tennessee Exemptions Relevant to Credit Card Debt

Homestead: $5,000 (individual) / $7,500 (joint) - protects home equity from credit card judgment liens

Vehicle: $10,000

Federal exemptions: Available in Tennessee as an alternative to state exemptions

Full Tennessee exemptions guide →

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I discharge credit card debt in Tennessee bankruptcy?

Yes. Credit card debt is unsecured debt and is generally dischargeable in both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 bankruptcy in Tennessee. In Chapter 7, it is eliminated entirely. In Chapter 13, you pay a percentage based on your disposable income over 3-5 years, and the rest is discharged.

Will recent credit card charges be discharged?

Charges of over $800 for luxury goods or services within 90 days of filing, and cash advances over $1,100 within 70 days of filing, are presumed non-dischargeable under 11 U.S.C. section 523(a)(2)(C). The creditor must object for this to apply.

Can creditors sue me for credit card debt?

Yes. In Tennessee, credit card companies can sue you, obtain a judgment, and garnish your wages or levy your bank account. Filing bankruptcy stops these actions through the automatic stay.

Ready to Explore Your Options?

Find out if you qualify for a bankruptcy discharge to eliminate your credit card debt.

Discharge Screener
Cost Calculator

## Using Credit Cards Before Bankruptcy in Tennessee
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/credit-cards-before-filing.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeMemphisNashvilleChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Credit Cards Before Filing

Using Credit Cards Before Bankruptcy in Tennessee

Stop using credit cards once you decide to file bankruptcy. Charges made near filing can be challenged as fraud - and the presumption works against you.

Quick answer: Do not use credit cards once you decide to file bankruptcy. Luxury purchases over $800 within 90 days and cash advances over $1,100 within 70 days are presumed fraudulent. These debts may survive your discharge.

The Two Presumption Periods

90-Day Rule: Luxury Goods

Applies to purchases of luxury goods/services

Threshold: $800+ to a single creditor

Within 90 days of filing

Presumed non-dischargeable (you must prove otherwise)

11 U.S.C. section 523(a)(2)(C)(i)(I)

70-Day Rule: Cash Advances

Applies to cash advances

Threshold: $1,100+ in aggregate

Within 70 days of filing

Presumed non-dischargeable (you must prove otherwise)

11 U.S.C. section 523(a)(2)(C)(i)(II)

What You Should Do

Stop Using Cards Immediately

The moment you decide to file bankruptcy, stop all credit card use. Here is why:

Every charge creates risk. Even necessary purchases can be challenged if made close to filing.

Intent matters. Using a credit card when you know you will not repay it is the definition of fraud in this context.

The burden shifts to you. Within the 90/70 day windows, YOU must prove the charges were not fraudulent. Outside those windows, the creditor must prove fraud.

What Charges Are Safe?

Groceries and essential food - not luxury, reasonably necessary

Gas for work commute - necessary for income

Medical expenses - necessary for health

Basic utilities - if auto-billed to the card, switch to bank payment

Even "safe" charges should be avoided if possible. Use cash or debit instead. The cleaner your pre-filing credit card activity, the smoother your case.

What Happens If Charges Are Challenged

If a credit card company believes you ran up charges with no intent to repay, they can file an adversary proceeding - a lawsuit within your bankruptcy case. The process:

Creditor files a complaint alleging non-dischargeability under section 523(a)(2)

You must respond within 30 days

Discovery phase: both sides exchange evidence (statements, purchase records)

Trial or settlement: most adversary proceedings settle before trial

If the creditor wins, those specific charges survive your discharge

Adversary proceedings are expensive and time-consuming for both sides. Creditors typically only file them for significant amounts ($2,000+). But avoiding the risk entirely is the smartest strategy.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I use credit cards before filing bankruptcy in Tennessee?

Technically you can, but it is strongly discouraged. Charges made with no intent to repay can be challenged as fraud. Luxury purchases over $800 within 90 days of filing are presumed fraudulent. Cash advances over $1,100 within 70 days are also presumed fraudulent.

What is the 90-day rule for credit cards before bankruptcy?

Under 11 U.S.C. section 523(a)(2)(C), charges for luxury goods or services exceeding $800 made within 90 days of filing bankruptcy are presumed to be non-dischargeable. The creditor does not have to prove fraud - you must prove the charges were not fraudulent.

What is the 70-day rule for cash advances?

Cash advances exceeding $1,100 taken within 70 days of filing are presumed non-dischargeable under 11 U.S.C. section 523(a)(2)(C). This includes balance transfers, convenience checks, and ATM cash advances.

What counts as a luxury purchase?

Luxury goods and services are items not reasonably necessary for support or maintenance. Groceries, gas, medical expenses, and basic clothing are not luxury. Electronics, jewelry, vacations, expensive dining, and entertainment are typically considered luxury.

What happens if I used credit cards right before filing?

The credit card company may file an adversary proceeding arguing the charges are non-dischargeable due to fraud. If the charges are within the 90/70 day presumption periods, you bear the burden of proving they were not fraudulent. Charges outside those windows require the creditor to prove actual fraud.

Should I close my credit cards before filing bankruptcy?

Do not close accounts before filing - it does not help and can look like you are trying to hide something. Simply stop using the cards. They will be listed in your bankruptcy schedules and the issuers will close them after receiving notice of your filing.

Related Topics

Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Exemptions
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House
Credit After
Garnishment
FAQ

## Bankruptcy Disqualifications in Tennessee
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/disqualifications.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeMemphisNashvilleChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide›Disqualifications

Bankruptcy Disqualifications in Tennessee

Not everyone qualifies for bankruptcy relief. Understanding the disqualifications can help Tennessee residents plan their filing strategy.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Filing Bars: When You Cannot File

Prior Discharge Time Bars

Prior DischargeNew Chapter 7New Chapter 13

Chapter 78 years4 years

Chapter 136 years*2 years

*The 6-year bar for Ch.13-to-Ch.7 is waived if you paid 100% of unsecured claims or at least 70% in a good-faith plan.

Use the 1328(f) Screener to calculate your exact eligibility dates.

Grounds for Denial of Discharge

Under 11 U.S.C. section 727(a), the court can deny your Chapter 7 discharge entirely if:

Concealment or transfer of property with intent to defraud within 1 year before filing or during the case (section 727(a)(2))

Destruction or concealment of records relating to your financial condition (section 727(a)(3))

Perjury or false statements in connection with the case (section 727(a)(4))

Failure to explain loss of assets or deficiency of assets (section 727(a)(5))

Failure to complete the post-filing financial management course (section 727(a)(11))

Prior discharge within the applicable time period (section 727(a)(8) and (a)(9))

Credit Counseling Requirement

You cannot file bankruptcy in W.D. Tenn., M.D. Tenn., and E.D. Tenn. without first completing a credit counseling course from an approved provider within 180 days before filing. There is no exception except:

Exigent circumstances that merit a waiver (very rarely granted)

The approved providers for your district are unable to provide services (almost never happens)

Courses can be completed online, by phone, or in person and typically take about one hour. A second course (debtor education / financial management) is required after filing but before discharge.

180-Day Dismissal Bar

Under 11 U.S.C. section 109(g), you cannot file a new bankruptcy petition if a prior case was dismissed within the past 180 days for:

Willful failure to obey orders of the court

Willful failure to appear before the court

Filing a motion to dismiss after a creditor requested relief from the automatic stay

If you have had a recent dismissal, count the days carefully. Filing before the 180-day period expires can result in immediate dismissal of the new case.

Means Test Disqualification (Chapter 7 Only)

If your income is above the Tennessee median and you do not pass the means test expense deductions, you cannot file Chapter 7. Your options are:

File Chapter 13 instead (no income ceiling)

Wait until your income drops (e.g., job change, retirement)

Recalculate after 6 months if a high-income period falls out of the lookback window

Frequently Asked Questions

What disqualifies you from filing bankruptcy in Tennessee?

You may be disqualified from bankruptcy if: you did not complete credit counseling, you received a recent prior discharge (8 years for Ch.7-to-Ch.7, 4 years for Ch.7-to-Ch.13, 6 years for Ch.13-to-Ch.7, 2 years for Ch.13-to-Ch.13), you committed fraud, your prior case was dismissed within 180 days for cause, or your income is too high for Chapter 7 and debts exceed Chapter 13 limits.

Can I be denied a bankruptcy discharge?

Yes. Even if you are allowed to file, the court can deny discharge for cause under section 727(a). Grounds include concealing assets, destroying records, committing perjury, failing to explain loss of assets, or failing to complete the financial management course.

What happens if my bankruptcy was recently dismissed in Tennessee?

If a case was dismissed within the past 180 days for willful failure to obey a court order, appear before the court, or properly prosecute the case, you cannot file a new case immediately. Additionally, repeat filings within one year can limit or eliminate automatic stay protection.

Check Your Eligibility Now

Our free screener checks prior filing bars and time limits instantly.

## Divorce and Bankruptcy in Tennessee
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/divorce-and-bankruptcy.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeMemphisNashvilleChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide›Divorce & Bankruptcy

Divorce and Bankruptcy in Tennessee

Divorce and bankruptcy often occur together. Understanding how they interact in Tennessee can help you protect your assets, manage shared debts, and plan the best filing strategy.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Filing Order: Bankruptcy Before, During, or After Divorce

1

Before Divorce

Filing a joint bankruptcy before divorce can eliminate shared unsecured debt and simplify the divorce property settlement. You save on filing fees by filing one joint case instead of two individual cases.

2

During Divorce

You can file bankruptcy during divorce proceedings. The automatic stay will pause any debt-related orders but does not affect custody, support, or the divorce itself. Coordination between courts is needed.

3

After Divorce

Filing after divorce means you file individually. Your divorce decree does not override bankruptcy law - a creditor can still come after you for joint debts even if the decree assigns them to your ex-spouse.

What Bankruptcy Cannot Discharge in Divorce

Under 11 U.S.C. section 523(a)(5) and (a)(15), the following divorce-related debts are not dischargeable:

Child support - all arrears and ongoing obligations

Alimony / spousal support - all arrears and ongoing obligations

Property settlement obligations - debts to a former spouse under a divorce decree, separation agreement, or property settlement (non-dischargeable in Chapter 7; potentially dischargeable in Chapter 13)

Hold-harmless agreements - if you agreed to pay a joint debt in the divorce, that obligation survives Chapter 7

Key difference: In Chapter 13, property division debts (section 523(a)(15)) may be dischargeable, while domestic support obligations (section 523(a)(5)) are never dischargeable.

Joint Debts and the Co-Debtor Stay

When one spouse files Chapter 13, the co-debtor stay under 11 U.S.C. section 1301 temporarily protects the non-filing spouse from creditors trying to collect on joint consumer debts. This protection does not exist in Chapter 7.

This is particularly important in Tennessee where both spouses may be on the mortgage, car loan, or credit cards. The co-debtor stay lasts as long as the Chapter 13 plan is active.

Tennessee Property Considerations

Homestead exemption: $5,000 (individual) / $7,500 (joint) - the bankruptcy and divorce courts both consider home equity

Vehicle exemption: $10,000

Federal exemptions available: Tennessee allows choosing federal exemptions, which may be better depending on your assets

See the full Tennessee exemptions guide for details.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I file bankruptcy during a divorce in Tennessee?

Yes. You can file bankruptcy before, during, or after a divorce in Tennessee. The timing affects how debts and property are divided. Filing before the divorce can simplify property division by eliminating joint unsecured debt.

Does bankruptcy discharge alimony or child support?

No. Domestic support obligations including alimony and child support are not dischargeable in bankruptcy under any chapter. They are priority debts that must be paid in full.

What happens to joint debts in bankruptcy?

If one spouse files bankruptcy and discharges a joint debt, the other spouse remains fully liable. The creditor can pursue the non-filing spouse for the entire balance. A Chapter 13 co-debtor stay can temporarily protect the non-filing spouse during the repayment plan.

Planning Your Next Step

If you are considering bankruptcy alongside divorce, check your discharge eligibility first.

Discharge Screener
Chapter 7 Guide
