## Emergency Bankruptcy Filing in Tennessee
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/emergency-bankruptcy.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeMemphisNashvilleChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide›Emergency Filing

Emergency Bankruptcy Filing in Tennessee

When foreclosure, repossession, or garnishment is days or even hours away, Tennessee residents can file an emergency bankruptcy petition to trigger the automatic stay immediately.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

What Is an Emergency Bankruptcy Filing?

An emergency bankruptcy filing - sometimes called a bare-bones or skeleton filing - is when you file only the minimum required documents to open a case and activate the automatic stay. You file the rest within 14 days.

Minimum Documents Required

Voluntary Petition - the two-page form that officially opens the case

Creditor Matrix - a list of all known creditors with names and addresses

Credit Counseling Certificate - proof of the required pre-filing credit counseling course (can be completed online in about an hour)

When to Consider Emergency Filing in Tennessee

Emergency Filing Makes Sense When

A foreclosure sale is scheduled within days

Your employer received a garnishment order that starts next pay period

A creditor is actively repossessing your vehicle

A bank account levy is about to clear

A lawsuit judgment is about to be enforced

Utility shutoff is imminent

Better to File a Complete Petition When

You have several weeks before any deadline

You want to avoid the risk of missing the 14-day supplement deadline

Your financial situation is complex and needs careful preparation

You need time to gather all financial documents

The 14-Day Deadline

After an emergency filing in W.D. Tenn., M.D. Tenn., and E.D. Tenn., you have 14 days to file all remaining documents:

Schedules A/B through J (assets, liabilities, income, expenses)

Statement of Financial Affairs

Means Test calculation (Form 122A or 122C)

Tax returns for the most recent year

Pay stubs for the 60 days prior to filing

If you miss the 14-day deadline and do not receive an extension, the court will dismiss your case. This can limit the automatic stay protection in any future filing within one year.

Tennessee Exemption Quick Reference

When you complete your schedules, you will need to claim exemptions to protect your property:

Homestead: $5,000 (individual) / $7,500 (joint)

Vehicle: $10,000

Federal exemptions: Tennessee allows you to choose between state and federal exemption systems

For a complete list, see the Tennessee exemptions page.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I file an emergency bankruptcy in Tennessee?

Yes. You can file an emergency (bare-bones) bankruptcy petition in Tennessee to get the automatic stay protection immediately. You file only the two-page voluntary petition, a creditor list, and proof of credit counseling. You then have 14 days to file the remaining schedules and documents.

How fast can the automatic stay take effect in Tennessee?

The automatic stay takes effect the instant your petition is filed with the W.D. Tenn., M.D. Tenn., and E.D. Tenn. bankruptcy court. If you file electronically during court hours, it can happen within minutes. Emergency filings are designed for situations where a foreclosure sale, wage garnishment, or repossession is imminent.

What happens if I miss the 14-day deadline?

If you fail to file the remaining schedules and documents within 14 days, the court may dismiss your case. You can request a one-time extension, but it is not guaranteed. A dismissal could limit your ability to refile and get the automatic stay again.

Check Your Filing Eligibility

Before filing - emergency or otherwise - make sure you are eligible for a bankruptcy discharge.

## Bankruptcy and Employment in Tennessee - Discrimination, Hiring, Your Rights
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/employment.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostGlossaryFaqOBP

Home›Bankruptcy and Employment in Tennessee

Bankruptcy and Employment in Tennessee

Federal law protects you from employment discrimination based on bankruptcy. Government employers have strict prohibitions. Private employer protections are narrower but still significant. Here is what Tennessee workers need to know.

Quick answer: Under 11 U.S.C. section 525(a), government employers cannot fire, refuse to hire, or discriminate against you based on bankruptcy. Under section 525(b), private employers cannot fire you solely for filing. However, most courts hold that private employers CAN consider bankruptcy in hiring decisions. Some states add extra protections.

Federal Protections: Section 525

Section 525(a): Government Employers

Cannot terminate based on bankruptcy

Cannot refuse to hire based on bankruptcy

Cannot discriminate in any way based on bankruptcy

Covers federal, state, and local government

Also covers government-issued licenses and permits

Broad and well-established protection

Section 525(b): Private Employers

Cannot terminate solely because of bankruptcy

Hiring: Most courts say section 525(b) does NOT prohibit refusing to hire

The word "hire" was deliberately omitted from 525(b)

Cannot discriminate against current employees

Narrower than government employer protections

Some states fill the gap with credit check restrictions

Tennessee-Specific Employment Protections

11 U.S.C. section 525(a) protects government employees. Tennessee does not have a state statute specifically prohibiting private employer bankruptcy discrimination. The Tennessee Human Rights Act (Tenn. Code 4-21) prohibits discrimination on certain grounds but does not list bankruptcy. The 6th Circuit (covering Tennessee) has addressed section 525(b). Tennessee's defense/energy sector (Oak Ridge, Arnold AFB, Fort Campbell) creates significant clearance-relevant employment.

Credit Checks in Employment

The Fair Credit Reporting Act (FCRA) governs employer access to credit reports:

Written consent required: An employer must get your written permission before pulling your credit report

You can say no: But declining may affect your candidacy for positions requiring financial trust

Adverse action notice: If an employer takes adverse action based on your credit report, they must notify you and provide a copy of the report

Employment versions: Employers receive a modified credit report that does not include your credit score - they see payment history, accounts, and public records (including bankruptcy)

Several states and cities restrict employer use of credit checks. Check whether Tennessee has additional restrictions.

Jobs Most Affected by Bankruptcy

While most jobs are unaffected by bankruptcy, certain positions may be more sensitive:

Financial industry: Banks, investment firms, insurance companies may consider bankruptcy for positions handling money

Government with clearance: Security clearance positions are affected but not disqualified (see our security clearance page)

Law enforcement: Some agencies consider financial history

Corporate officers: C-suite positions may have different standards

Bonded positions: Jobs requiring a surety bond may be affected if the bonding company considers bankruptcy

For the vast majority of jobs - retail, healthcare, education, technology, manufacturing, service industry - bankruptcy has no effect on employment.

Professional Licenses in Tennessee

Under section 525(a), Tennessee licensing authorities cannot deny, revoke, or refuse to renew professional licenses solely because of bankruptcy. This includes:

Real estate licenses

Medical and nursing licenses

Legal licenses (bar admission)

Teaching certificates

Trade licenses (plumbing, electrical, etc.)

Insurance agent licenses

If a licensing board requires a "financial responsibility" showing, the underlying financial condition (not the bankruptcy filing itself) is what matters. Successfully completing bankruptcy actually demonstrates financial responsibility.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I be fired for filing bankruptcy?

Government employers: absolutely not. Private employers: cannot fire you solely for bankruptcy under section 525(b). Using it as one factor among many is a gray area.

Can an employer refuse to hire me?

Government employers cannot. Most courts hold private employers can consider bankruptcy in hiring. Some states restrict credit check use in hiring, providing indirect protection.

Can my employer check my credit?

Only with your written consent under the FCRA. You can decline, but it may affect your candidacy for financial positions.

Does bankruptcy affect professional licenses?

Government licensing authorities cannot deny or revoke licenses based on bankruptcy under section 525(a). Financial responsibility requirements may still apply.

Should I tell my employer I filed?

Generally not required unless you are in a position with financial disclosure requirements. If wage garnishment stops because of bankruptcy, your employer may become aware. Focus on your job performance.

Related Topics

Exemptions
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House
Credit After
Wage Garnishment
Medical Debt

## Can I Keep My Jewelry in Tennessee Bankruptcy?
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/engagement-ring.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeMemphisNashvilleChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Jewelry Exemptions

Can I Keep My Jewelry in Tennessee Bankruptcy?

Your engagement ring, wedding band, and other jewelry may be protected by Tennessee exemptions. Here is what the law says and how values are determined.

Quick answer: Tennessee has no specific jewelry exemption. However, Tennessee offers a $10,000 wildcard exemption under Tenn. Code 26-2-103 that can be applied to any property, including jewelry. This is one of the most generous wildcard exemptions in the country. Tennessee does not allow federal exemptions.

Tennessee Jewelry Exemption Details

Tennessee has no specific jewelry exemption. However, Tennessee offers a $10,000 wildcard exemption under Tenn. Code 26-2-103 that can be applied to any property, including jewelry. This is one of the most generous wildcard exemptions in the country. Tennessee does not allow federal exemptions.

The exemption amount is based on the jewelry's replacement value - what a comparable item would cost at retail, not what a pawnshop or jeweler would pay you. In practice, most everyday jewelry (wedding rings, watches, earrings) falls well within the exemption limits.

How Jewelry Is Valued

Replacement value standard: The Bankruptcy Code uses replacement value - the price a retail buyer would pay for a comparable item in similar condition.

Not purchase price: What you paid is irrelevant. A ring purchased for $5,000 may have a replacement value of $2,000-$3,000.

Not resale value: Pawn shop or "we buy gold" prices are lower than replacement value and are not the correct standard.

Professional appraisal: If you have valuable jewelry, get a written appraisal from a certified appraiser. The appraiser should provide a replacement value for insurance purposes.

Trustee assessment: The Chapter 7 trustee may request an appraisal if they believe jewelry values are understated. Most trustees do not pursue jewelry worth less than $1,000-$2,000 above exemption limits.

Practical Reality: Trustees and Jewelry

In practice, bankruptcy trustees rarely pursue jewelry unless its value significantly exceeds the exemption amount. Here is why:

Transaction costs: Selling jewelry at auction or through consignment involves fees that reduce the net recovery for creditors.

Low recovery rates: Used jewelry typically sells for 20-40% of retail replacement value at liquidation.

De minimis recovery: If the net benefit to creditors is less than a few hundred dollars, most trustees determine the sale is not worth the effort.

Emotional factor: Trustees understand that taking wedding rings creates bad optics and may exercise discretion.

Important: Always disclose all jewelry honestly on your schedules. Hiding assets is fraud. If your jewelry is within the exemption, the trustee will not take it. If it is close to the limit, the trustee will likely not pursue it.

Protecting Jewelry That Exceeds the Exemption

If your jewelry exceeds Tennessee's exemption, you may have options:

Wildcard exemption: If Tennessee offers a wildcard (general property) exemption, you can stack it on top of the jewelry exemption to protect additional value.

Chapter 13: In Chapter 13, you keep all your property. Instead of surrendering jewelry, you pay creditors the equivalent value through your plan. This is often the better option for people with valuable jewelry.

Negotiate with trustee: You may be able to pay the trustee the non-exempt value in cash to keep the jewelry - called "buying back" the asset from the estate.

Federal exemptions: If Tennessee allows the federal exemptions (11 U.S.C. 522(d)), you may get a higher jewelry exemption ($1,875) plus a significant wildcard ($1,475 + up to $13,950 of unused homestead).

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I keep my engagement ring in Tennessee bankruptcy?

It depends on the ring's value and Tennessee's exemptions. No specific jewelry exemption - use $10,000 wildcard (Tenn. Code 26-2-103). If your ring's value is below the exemption limit, you can keep it. If it exceeds the limit, you may need to use additional exemptions (like a wildcard) to protect it.

What is the jewelry exemption in Tennessee?

No specific jewelry exemption - use $10,000 wildcard (Tenn. Code 26-2-103). This applies to all jewelry including engagement rings, wedding bands, watches, and other personal adornment. Values are based on resale/liquidation value, not what you paid.

How is jewelry valued in bankruptcy?

Jewelry is valued at replacement value - what it would cost to buy a comparable item at retail, not what a jeweler would pay you. In practice, bankruptcy trustees often accept values significantly below retail. A professional appraisal can help establish the value.

What if my jewelry is worth more than the exemption?

If your jewelry exceeds the exemption limit, you may be able to protect the excess using a wildcard exemption (if available in your state). Otherwise, the trustee may seek to sell the jewelry and return the exempt portion to you. In practice, trustees rarely pursue jewelry unless the value significantly exceeds exemptions.

Do I have to disclose all jewelry in bankruptcy?

Yes. All assets must be disclosed on Schedule A/B, including jewelry. Failure to disclose assets is bankruptcy fraud and can result in denial of discharge, fines, or criminal prosecution. Always disclose - even if you believe the item is fully exempt.

What about inherited jewelry or family heirlooms?

Inherited jewelry and heirlooms are valued the same way as any other jewelry - replacement value. Sentimental value does not factor into the financial analysis. However, trustees rarely pursue items with modest resale value, even if sentimental value is high.

Related Topics

Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Exemptions
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House
Credit After
Garnishment

## Can Bankruptcy Stop an Eviction in Tennessee?
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/eviction.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeMemphisNashvilleChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Eviction

Can Bankruptcy Stop an Eviction in Tennessee?

Timing is everything. Bankruptcy can halt an eviction if you file before a judgment for possession is entered. After a judgment, the protection is limited.

Quick answer: If no eviction judgment has been entered, filing bankruptcy immediately stops the eviction through the automatic stay. If a judgment already exists, section 362(b)(22) allows the landlord to proceed. File before the judgment to maximize protection.

Before vs. After Eviction Judgment

Before Judgment

Automatic stay stops eviction

Landlord cannot proceed in state court

Chapter 13 can cure back rent over 3-5 years

You must pay post-petition rent going forward

Emergency filing possible in hours

After Judgment

Section 362(b)(22) limits protection

Landlord can proceed with eviction

Exception: if state law allows cure after judgment, you may file certification under 362(l)

Most Tennessee courts apply this strictly

Chapter 7 will discharge the rent debt but not stop eviction

Tennessee Eviction Timeline

Governing law: Tenn. Code 29-18-101 et seq.

Tennessee requires a 14-day notice for nonpayment (30 days for lease violations). Detainer warrant cases are heard in General Sessions Court, typically within 6-10 days of filing.

Understanding this timeline is critical for deciding when to file bankruptcy. Once the landlord obtains a judgment for possession, your bankruptcy protections are significantly reduced.

Key window: File bankruptcy BEFORE the judge enters a judgment for possession. Once a judgment is entered, section 362(b)(22) may allow the eviction to continue despite your filing.

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 for Evictions

Chapter 7

Discharges back rent (pre-petition)

Automatic stay is temporary (case lasts 3-4 months)

Does not allow you to cure arrears

Best if you plan to move anyway

Eliminates personal liability for lease balance

Chapter 13

Allows curing back rent through plan

Automatic stay lasts 3-5 years (plan duration)

You must assume the lease and stay current

Best if you want to keep your housing

Plan payments include arrears catch-up

Emergency Filing to Stop Eviction

If eviction is imminent, an emergency bankruptcy filing can be done quickly:

Bare-bones petition: File just the petition, Statement of Social Security Number, and credit counseling certificate. You have 14 days to file the remaining schedules.

Immediate protection: The automatic stay takes effect the moment the petition is filed with the bankruptcy court - not when the landlord is notified.

Proof of filing: Get a copy of the filed petition with the case number and give it to your landlord and the state court handling the eviction.

Filing fee: Chapter 7 costs $338 and Chapter 13 costs $313. Fee waivers are available for those below 150% of the poverty line.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can bankruptcy stop an eviction in Tennessee?

It depends on timing. If no eviction judgment has been entered, filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay, which immediately halts eviction proceedings. If a judgment for possession already exists, section 362(b)(22) limits the stay's protection, and the landlord may be able to proceed.

What is section 362(b)(22)?

Section 362(b)(22) of the Bankruptcy Code is an exception to the automatic stay. If a landlord obtained a judgment for possession before you filed bankruptcy, the stay does not prevent the landlord from continuing the eviction. This was added by the 2005 BAPCPA amendments to prevent abuse of bankruptcy to delay evictions.

What if I file bankruptcy before the eviction judgment in Tennessee?

If no judgment for possession has been entered, the automatic stay stops the eviction cold. The landlord cannot proceed with the eviction, change locks, or remove your property. However, you must still pay post-petition rent - the stay does not excuse future rent payments.

Can I cure a lease default in Chapter 13?

In Chapter 13, you can cure lease defaults (back rent) through your repayment plan. You assume the lease and pay arrears over the plan period (3-5 years). This only works if your landlord has not yet obtained a judgment for possession.

Does filing bankruptcy erase back rent?

Chapter 7 can discharge back rent owed before filing, but it does not stop an eviction if a judgment already exists. Chapter 13 lets you cure arrears while keeping your home. Post-petition rent must always be paid regardless of the chapter filed.

How quickly can I file bankruptcy to stop an eviction in Tennessee?

Emergency (bare-bones) bankruptcy filings can be done in hours. You file the petition with minimal schedules and have 14 days to complete the rest. The automatic stay takes effect the moment the petition is filed with the court.

Related Topics

Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Exemptions
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House
Credit After
Garnishment

## Tennessee Bankruptcy Exemptions - What You Can Keep
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/exemptions.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQStatsResourcesOBP

Home›Exemptions

Tennessee Bankruptcy Exemptions

Exemptions determine what property you get to keep when you file bankruptcy. Understanding Tennessee's exemptions is critical - they can mean the difference between keeping and losing your home, car, and savings.

What Are Exemptions?

Bankruptcy exemptions are laws that protect certain property from being taken to pay your creditors. In Chapter 7, non-exempt property can be sold by the trustee. In Chapter 13, the value of non-exempt property affects how much you must pay through your plan.

Tennessee allows filers to choose between state and federal exemptions. Many filers compare both to determine which provides better protection for their specific assets.

Tennessee State Exemptions - Full List

Homestead - $5,000 / $7,500

Tennessee's homestead exemption protects up to $5,000 of equity in your primary residence for an individual, or $7,500 for a married couple or if you are 62 or older. Tennessee also allows filers to choose federal exemptions instead.

Tenn. Code Ann. 26-2-301

Motor Vehicle - No specific vehicle exemption under state law (federal: $4,450)

Protects equity in one motor vehicle up to the exemption limit. If you owe more than the car is worth (you are "underwater"), there is no equity to protect and the exemption is not needed.

Other Tennessee Exemptions

PropertyAmount

Household goods, furnishings$4,000 total (state)

ClothingIncluded in household goods

Tools of trade$1,900

Jewelry$1,500

Wildcard$10,000 (state wildcard); federal: $1,475 + up to $13,950 unused homestead

Retirement accounts (IRA, 401k, pension)Fully exempt

Social Security benefitsFully exempt

Workers' compensationFully exempt

Public assistance benefitsFully exempt

Federal Exemption Alternative

PropertyFederal Exemption

Homestead$27,900

Vehicle$4,450

Household goods$700 per item / $14,875 total

Wildcard$1,475 + up to $13,950 unused homestead

Tools of trade$2,800

Retirement accountsFully exempt

Special Notes

Tennessee allows filers to choose between state and federal exemptions. Most Tennessee filers choose federal exemptions because the state homestead exemption is very low, and the federal wildcard provides more flexibility.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the Tennessee homestead exemption?

Tennessee's homestead exemption protects up to $5,000 of equity in your primary residence for an individual, or $7,500 for a married couple or if you are 62 or older. Tennessee also allows filers to choose federal exemptions instead.

How much vehicle equity can I protect in Tennessee?

Tennessee's motor vehicle exemption is No specific vehicle exemption under state law (federal: $4,450). If your car equity is within that limit, you keep it in Chapter 7.

Does Tennessee allow federal exemptions?

Yes. Tennessee allows filers to choose between state and federal exemptions.

## Tennessee Bankruptcy FAQ - 15 Common Questions Answered
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/faq.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQStatsResourcesOBP

Home›Faq

Tennessee Bankruptcy FAQ

Answers to the 15 most common questions from Tennessee residents considering bankruptcy.

Cost and Eligibility

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Tennessee?

Chapter 7 filing fee is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Attorney fees typically range from $900 to $1,800 for Chapter 7 and $2,500 to $4,000 for Chapter 13. Credit counseling costs about $25-50 total. Chapter 13 fees can be paid through your repayment plan. Fee waivers available for low-income Chapter 7 filers.

Will I lose my house if I file bankruptcy in Tennessee?

Tennessee's homestead exemption protects up to $5,000 / $7,500 of equity in your home. In Chapter 13, you can keep your home and catch up on missed mortgage payments through your plan. Most homeowners keep their homes in bankruptcy.

Can I keep my car in bankruptcy in Tennessee?

Tennessee protects No specific vehicle exemption under state law (federal: $4,450) in vehicle equity. If your car equity is within the exemption limit, you keep it in Chapter 7. In Chapter 13, you can keep your car and catch up on payments through your plan.

How long does bankruptcy take?

Chapter 7 takes 3-4 months from filing to discharge. Chapter 13 requires completing a 3-5 year repayment plan. The 341 meeting of creditors happens about 30 days after filing.

Will bankruptcy stop wage garnishment?

Yes. The automatic stay takes effect the moment your petition is filed and immediately stops wage garnishments, bank levies, lawsuits, foreclosures, and repossessions.

Should I file Chapter 7 or Chapter 13?

Chapter 7 is faster (3-4 months) and eliminates most unsecured debt, but requires passing a means test. Chapter 13 lets you keep all property and catch up on secured debt arrears, but requires 3-5 years of payments. Your income, assets, and goals determine the best choice.

Where do I file for bankruptcy in Tennessee?

Tennessee has 3 districts: W.D. Tenn., M.D. Tenn., E.D. Tenn.. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

Will bankruptcy affect my credit score?

Yes. Chapter 7 stays on your credit report for 10 years and Chapter 13 for 7 years. However, many filers see credit scores start recovering within 1-2 years as debt-to-income ratios improve.

Can I file bankruptcy on medical bills?

Yes. Medical bills are unsecured debts that are fully dischargeable in both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13. Medical debt is one of the most common reasons people file bankruptcy.

Will bankruptcy eliminate my student loans?

Generally no. Student loans are not dischargeable except in cases of undue hardship, which requires a separate adversary proceeding and a difficult legal standard.

What is the means test?

The means test determines whether you qualify for Chapter 7. It compares your household income to the Tennessee median. If below, you qualify automatically. If above, a detailed expense calculation determines eligibility.

Can creditors still call me after I file?

No. The automatic stay prohibits all collection activity, including phone calls, letters, and emails. Creditors who violate the stay can be held in contempt and ordered to pay damages.

What is the 341 meeting of creditors?

A brief hearing about 30 days after filing where the bankruptcy trustee asks you questions under oath about your finances. Bring photo ID and Social Security proof. Most meetings last 5-10 minutes.

Can I file bankruptcy more than once?

Yes, but there are time limits. After a Chapter 7 discharge, you must wait 8 years for another Chapter 7 or 4 years for Chapter 13. After a Chapter 13 discharge, wait 6 years for Chapter 7 or 2 years for another Chapter 13.

What is the automatic stay?

The automatic stay is a court order that takes effect immediately when you file bankruptcy. It stops lawsuits, garnishments, foreclosures, repossessions, and collection calls.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

Free Discharge Screener

Open Bankruptcy Project Network

OBP Home

Ch. 7 vs 13
Automatic Stay
341 Meeting

## How to Find Bankruptcy Filings in Tennessee
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/find-bankruptcy-filing.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeMemphisNashvilleChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Find Filings

How to Find Bankruptcy Filings in Tennessee

Bankruptcy filings are public records. Here is how to search them in Tennessee - from free options to the official PACER system.

Quick answer: Search PACER at pacer.uscourts.gov ($0.10/page, $3 cap per doc). For free access, use CourtListener.com (RECAP archive) or call the Eastern, Middle, and Western Districts of Tennessee clerk's office. The Tennessee bankruptcy court website is https://www.tnmb.uscourts.gov.

Option 1: PACER (Official System)

PACER (Public Access to Court Electronic Records) is the federal court system's official electronic records platform.

Website: pacer.uscourts.gov

Cost: $0.10 per page, capped at $3.00 per document. Quarterly charges under $30 are waived.

Search by: Name, case number, Social Security number (last 4), date range

Tennessee court: https://ecf.tnmb.uscourts.gov

Registration: Free to create an account. Takes about 5 minutes.

1

Register

Create a free PACER account at pacer.uscourts.gov

2

Search

Use the national case locator or go directly to the Tennessee bankruptcy court

3

View

View docket entries and download documents ($0.10/page)

Option 2: RECAP / CourtListener (Free)

RECAP is a free browser extension that contributes PACER documents to a public archive. CourtListener hosts this archive and makes it searchable.

Website: courtlistener.com

Cost: Completely free

Coverage: Millions of documents, growing daily. Not every document is available, but many are.

Browser extension: Install RECAP for Chrome or Firefox. When you view PACER documents, RECAP automatically saves a copy to the public archive.

Option 3: Court Clerk's Office

You can contact the Eastern, Middle, and Western Districts of Tennessee bankruptcy court clerk's office directly:

Phone: Call the clerk and request a case search by name or case number. Basic case information (filing date, chapter, status) is typically provided for free.

In person: Visit the courthouse and use the public access terminals. No PACER account needed.

Website: https://www.tnmb.uscourts.gov may have a free case search or CM/ECF lookup

What Information Is Available

Docket sheet: Every document filed in the case, with dates and descriptions

Petition: Debtor's name, address, chapter filed, attorney

Schedules: Assets, liabilities, income, expenses (with sensitive info redacted)

Claims register: Every creditor claim filed in the case

Orders: Court orders, discharge, dismissal

Meeting of creditors (341): Date and outcome

Sensitive information is redacted: full Social Security numbers, complete financial account numbers, and the names of minor children are not visible in public filings.

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I find bankruptcy filings in Tennessee?

Bankruptcy filings in Tennessee are public records available through PACER (Public Access to Court Electronic Records) at pacer.uscourts.gov. You can search by name, case number, or Social Security number. The Eastern, Middle, and Western Districts of Tennessee has its electronic filing system at https://ecf.tnmb.uscourts.gov.

Is PACER free?

PACER charges $0.10 per page viewed, capped at $3.00 per document. Quarterly fees under $30 are waived. You can also use RECAP (a free browser extension) to access previously downloaded documents at no cost via CourtListener.com.

Can I search bankruptcy filings for free?

Yes. CourtListener.com (via the RECAP archive) has millions of free bankruptcy documents. Many court websites also offer free case information lookups. The bankruptcy court clerk's office will search records for free over the phone for basic information.

Which courts handle bankruptcy in Tennessee?

Bankruptcy cases in Tennessee are filed in the Eastern, Middle, and Western Districts of Tennessee. The court website is https://www.tnmb.uscourts.gov. PACER electronic filing is at https://ecf.tnmb.uscourts.gov.

How long are bankruptcy records available?

Bankruptcy records on PACER are generally available for the life of the case plus several years. Older cases may be archived but still accessible. Bankruptcy stays on your credit report for 7 years (Chapter 13) or 10 years (Chapter 7) from the filing date.

Can I see someone else's bankruptcy filing?

Yes. Bankruptcy filings are public records. Anyone can search PACER or visit the court clerk to view case documents. Some sensitive information (Social Security numbers, financial account numbers) is redacted from public filings.

Related Topics

Chapter 13

Keep Your Car

## Tennessee Bankruptcy Glossary
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/glossary.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeMemphisNashvilleChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide›Glossary

Tennessee Bankruptcy Glossary

Key bankruptcy terms and definitions for Tennessee filers, from automatic stay to wildcard exemption.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Bankruptcy Terms A-Z

341 Meeting (Meeting of Creditors)
A required hearing where the bankruptcy trustee (and any creditors who choose to attend) asks you questions under oath about your finances, assets, and petition. Held about 30 days after filing in W.D. Tenn., M.D. Tenn., and E.D. Tenn..

Adversary Proceeding
A lawsuit within a bankruptcy case, often filed to challenge the dischargeability of a specific debt or to recover fraudulent transfers.

Automatic Stay
A court order that takes effect immediately upon filing, stopping most collection actions, lawsuits, foreclosures, repossessions, and garnishments. See 11 U.S.C. section 362.

Chapter 7
Liquidation bankruptcy that eliminates most unsecured debt in 3-4 months. A trustee may sell non-exempt assets to pay creditors. Requires passing the means test.

Chapter 13
Reorganization bankruptcy for individuals with regular income. Debtor proposes a 3-5 year repayment plan to catch up on secured debts and pay a portion of unsecured debts.

Cramdown
Reducing the amount owed on a secured debt to the current value of the collateral. Available in Chapter 13 for vehicles purchased more than 910 days before filing and other secured debts.

Credit Counseling
A required course from an approved provider that must be completed within 180 days before filing bankruptcy. A second course (debtor education) is required before discharge.

Creditor
Any person or entity to whom you owe money. Includes secured creditors (backed by collateral), unsecured creditors (no collateral), and priority creditors (taxes, support obligations).

Discharge
A court order that permanently eliminates your personal liability for dischargeable debts. The discharge injunction (section 524) prevents creditors from collecting on discharged debts forever.

Disposable Income
Income remaining after subtracting allowed expenses. In Chapter 13, disposable income determines your plan payment amount.

Exemptions
Laws that protect specific property from being taken by the trustee in Chapter 7 or reduce what you pay in Chapter 13. Tennessee homestead exemption: $5,000 (individual) / $7,500 (joint); vehicle: $10,000.

Fraudulent Transfer
A transfer of property made to hinder, delay, or defraud creditors. The trustee can recover such transfers made within 2 years before filing (or longer under state law).

Lien
A legal claim on property as security for a debt. Common types include mortgage liens, auto liens, tax liens, and judgment liens.

Lien Stripping
Removing a wholly unsecured junior mortgage in Chapter 13. If the home value is less than the first mortgage balance, the second mortgage can be treated as unsecured debt.

Means Test
A formula under section 707(b) that determines Chapter 7 eligibility based on income and expenses. Compares your income to Tennessee median income for your household size.

Motion for Relief from Stay
A request by a creditor asking the court to lift the automatic stay, usually to allow foreclosure or repossession to proceed.

Non-Dischargeable Debt
Debts that survive bankruptcy, including student loans (absent undue hardship), most taxes, child support, alimony, debts from fraud, and DUI-related debts.

Petition
The official document filed with the bankruptcy court to start a case. Includes your personal information, chapter selected, and basic financial data.

Plan (Chapter 13)
A proposal showing how you will repay creditors over 3-5 years. Must pay priority debts in full, cure arrears on secured debts, and pay unsecured creditors at least what they would receive in Chapter 7.

Priority Debt
Debts that must be paid in full in Chapter 13 and cannot be discharged in Chapter 7. Includes recent taxes, child support, alimony, and certain government fines.

Proof of Claim
A form filed by a creditor establishing the amount and type of debt owed. Required for the creditor to receive payment in the case.

Reaffirmation Agreement
A voluntary agreement to remain liable for a debt after bankruptcy, typically used to keep a car or other secured property. Must be filed with the court.

Schedules
Detailed forms listing all assets, debts, income, expenses, and recent financial transactions. Required within 14 days of filing.

Secured Debt
A debt backed by collateral (like a mortgage or car loan). The creditor can repossess the collateral if the debt is not paid.

Trustee
A court-appointed official who administers your bankruptcy case. Chapter 7 trustees liquidate non-exempt assets. Chapter 13 trustees collect and distribute plan payments.

Unsecured Debt
A debt not backed by collateral, such as credit cards, medical bills, and personal loans. Generally dischargeable in bankruptcy.

Wildcard Exemption
An exemption that can be applied to any property type. Available under both state and federal exemption systems in Tennessee.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the difference between Chapter 7 and Chapter 13?

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debt in 3-4 months through liquidation of non-exempt assets. Chapter 13 is a 3-5 year repayment plan for people with regular income. Chapter 7 has income limits (means test); Chapter 13 has debt limits ($2,750,000).

What does discharge mean in bankruptcy?

A discharge is a permanent court order that eliminates your personal liability for specified debts. After discharge, creditors can never collect on those debts again. Not all debts are dischargeable - student loans, most taxes, child support, and alimony typically survive.

Learn More About Bankruptcy in Tennessee

Chapter 7 Guide
Chapter 13 Guide

## Bankruptcy Income Limits in Tennessee
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/income-limits.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeMemphisNashvilleChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide›Income Limits

Bankruptcy Income Limits in Tennessee

Your income determines which bankruptcy chapter you qualify for in Tennessee and how much you pay in Chapter 13. Here is how the income limits work.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Chapter 7 Income Limits

Chapter 7 uses the means test to determine eligibility. The test compares your household's current monthly income (averaged over the prior 6 months) to the Tennessee median income for your household size.

If Below Median Income

You automatically pass the means test and qualify for Chapter 7. No further calculation is needed.

If Above Median Income

You must complete the full means test, which deducts allowed expenses from your income. If the remaining disposable income falls below the threshold, you still qualify. The means test uses IRS expense standards plus your actual secured debt payments.

Tennessee median income figures are updated twice per year (April and October) by the U.S. Trustee Program. Check bankruptcymeanstest.org for current figures.

Chapter 13 - No Income Ceiling

Chapter 13 has no maximum income limit. Anyone with regular income can file, regardless of how high that income is. However, your income affects:

Plan length: Above-median income requires a 5-year plan; below-median allows 3-5 years

Payment amount: Higher income means higher monthly plan payments

Debt limits: Total debts (secured plus unsecured) cannot exceed $2,750,000

Chapter 13 requires regular income - wages, salary, self-employment, Social Security, pension, or other consistent sources.

Income Calculation Details

Current monthly income (CMI) includes almost all income from all sources over the 6 months before filing:

Wages, salary, overtime, bonuses, commissions

Self-employment income (gross receipts minus ordinary business expenses)

Interest, dividends, rental income

Pension, retirement, annuity payments

Unemployment compensation

Contributions from household members (spouse, non-filing partner)

Excluded from CMI: Social Security benefits, payments to victims of war crimes or terrorism.

Frequently Asked Questions

What are the income limits for bankruptcy in Tennessee?

For Chapter 7, your household income must be at or below the Tennessee median for your household size, or you must pass the means test expense deduction. Chapter 13 has no income ceiling - anyone with regular income can file. However, Chapter 13 has a debt limit of $2,750,000.

What if my income is too high for Chapter 7?

If your income exceeds the Tennessee median, you still might qualify through the means test expense deductions. If not, Chapter 13 allows any income level - higher income just means higher plan payments over 3-5 years.

Does household size affect income limits?

Yes. The median income threshold increases with household size. A single filer has a lower threshold than a family of four. Household size typically includes everyone living in the home and dependents you support.

Check Your Eligibility

The means test and discharge screener can help determine your options.

Discharge Screener

## Tennessee Bankruptcy - Chapter 7 & 13 Filing Guide [2026]
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/

Skip to main content

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQStatsResourcesOBP Rule 9037 PII Scanner

Tennessee Bankruptcy - Your Complete Filing Guide for 2026

You are not alone. Tennessee is among the top ten states for bankruptcy filings per capita, with particularly high rates in the Memphis area. This free guide covers everything Tennessee residents need to know about filing bankruptcy, from exemptions to costs to what happens after your case is filed.

Read the FAQ
Check Your Eligibility

$5,000 / $7,500

Homestead Exemption

3

Federal Districts

$338

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$313

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13: Which Is Right for You?

Chapter 7 - Fresh Start

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts (credit cards, medical bills, personal loans) in about 3-4 months. You must pass a means test based on your income.

Discharge in 3-4 months

Eliminates credit cards, medical debt

Must pass the means test

May lose non-exempt property

Filing fee: $338

Learn more about Chapter 7 →

Chapter 13 - Payment Plan

Chapter 13 lets you keep your property while repaying debts over 3-5 years. Good for saving a home from foreclosure or a car from repossession. No means test required.

3-5 year repayment plan

Keep your home and car

Catch up on missed payments

No means test requirement

Filing fee: $313

Learn more about Chapter 13 →

Not sure which chapter fits? Compare them in detail.

Tennessee Federal Court Districts

Tennessee has 3 federal bankruptcy districts. Where you live determines where you file.

Western District of Tennessee (W.D. Tenn.)

over 80,000 cases

Courthouse: 200 Jefferson Ave, Suite 413, Memphis, TN 38103

Phone: (901) 328-3500 | tnwb.uscourts.gov

Covers Memphis and western Tennessee. Among the highest filing rates in the state.

Middle District of Tennessee (M.D. Tenn.)

over 70,000 cases

Courthouse: 701 Broadway, Nashville, TN 37203

Phone: (615) 736-5584 | tnmb.uscourts.gov

Covers Nashville and central Tennessee.

Eastern District of Tennessee (E.D. Tenn.)

over 60,000 cases

Courthouse: 800 Market St, Suite 330, Knoxville, TN 37902

Phone: (865) 545-4279 | tneb.uscourts.gov

Covers Knoxville, Chattanooga, and eastern Tennessee.

Tennessee Exemptions at a Glance

Tennessee's homestead exemption protects up to $5,000 of equity in your primary residence for an individual, or $7,500 for a married couple or if you are 62 or older. Tennessee also allows filers to choose federal exemptions instead.

Tennessee allows filers to choose between state and federal exemptions, which can be advantageous depending on your situation.

PropertyTennessee Exemption

Homestead$5,000 / $7,500

VehicleNo specific vehicle exemption under state law (federal: $4,450)

Household goods$4,000 total (state)

Wildcard$10,000 (state wildcard); federal: $1,475 + up to $13,950 unused homestead

Retirement (IRA/401k)Fully exempt

Social SecurityFully exempt

Full exemption details →

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Tennessee?

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338 (can request installment payments)

Chapter 13 filing fee: $313 (can be paid through your plan)

Attorney fees: Typically $900 to $1,800 for Chapter 7; $2,500 to $4,000 for Chapter 13 in Tennessee

Credit counseling: $15-25 per course (two required)

Fee waivers are available for Chapter 7 filers whose income is below 150% of the federal poverty guidelines.

Full cost breakdown → | National cost guide

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does bankruptcy cost in Tennessee?

Chapter 7 filing fees are $338 and Chapter 13 filing fees are $313. Attorney fees in Tennessee typically range from $900 to $1,800 for Chapter 7 and $2,500 to $4,000 for Chapter 13. You will also need to pay for two required credit counseling courses, usually $25-$50 total. Full cost breakdown.

Will I lose my house if I file bankruptcy in Tennessee?

Tennessee's homestead exemption protects up to $5,000 / $7,500 of equity in your home. If your equity is below that amount, you can keep your home in Chapter 7. In Chapter 13, you can keep your home while catching up on missed payments through your repayment plan. See all exemptions.

How long does bankruptcy take?

Chapter 7 typically takes 3-4 months from filing to discharge. Chapter 13 requires completing a 3-5 year repayment plan before receiving a discharge. The 341 meeting of creditors usually happens about 30 days after filing.

Should I file Chapter 7 or Chapter 13?

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debt quickly but requires passing a means test. Chapter 13 allows you to keep property while repaying debts over 3-5 years. Your income, assets, and type of debt determine which chapter is best. Compare chapters.

Where do I file for bankruptcy in Tennessee?

Tennessee has 3 federal bankruptcy districts: W.D. Tenn., M.D. Tenn., E.D. Tenn.. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

See all frequently asked questions →

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

## Judgments and Bankruptcy in Tennessee
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/judgments.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeMemphisNashvilleChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide›Judgments

Judgments and Bankruptcy in Tennessee

A civil judgment against you does not mean you are out of options. Bankruptcy can discharge the underlying debt and even remove judgment liens from your property in Tennessee.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Discharging Judgments in Bankruptcy

Whether a judgment is dischargeable depends on the type of debt behind it:

Generally Dischargeable

Credit card judgments

Medical debt judgments

Personal loan judgments

Breach of contract judgments

Deficiency judgments (auto, mortgage)

Generally NOT Dischargeable

Judgments based on fraud

Judgments for willful injury

DUI/DWI damage judgments

Domestic support judgments

Student loan judgments (absent hardship)

Government fine/penalty judgments

Removing Judgment Liens Under Section 522(f)

Even when a judgment is discharged, the lien it created on your property may survive unless you take action. Under 11 U.S.C. section 522(f), you can file a motion to avoid (remove) a judicial lien if it impairs your exemptions.

How It Works in Tennessee

Home: If your home equity minus the judgment lien and any mortgages is less than the $5,000 (individual) / $7,500 (joint) homestead exemption, the lien impairs your exemption and can be avoided

Vehicle: Same analysis using the $10,000 vehicle exemption

Other property: Any exempt property with an impairing judgment lien qualifies

You must file a separate motion with the W.D. Tenn., M.D. Tenn., and E.D. Tenn. court to avoid the lien. The discharge alone does not remove it.

Stopping Judgment Enforcement

The moment you file bankruptcy, the automatic stay immediately stops all judgment enforcement:

Wage garnishment must stop

Bank account levies are frozen

Property liens cannot be enforced

No new collection lawsuits can be filed

Existing lawsuits are paused

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I discharge a judgment in bankruptcy in Tennessee?

Yes. Most money judgments based on unsecured debt (credit cards, medical bills, personal loans) are dischargeable in bankruptcy in Tennessee. The underlying debt determines dischargeability - if the original debt was dischargeable, the judgment based on that debt is also dischargeable.

Can I remove a judgment lien from my property?

Yes. Under 11 U.S.C. section 522(f), you can avoid (remove) a judicial lien on your property to the extent it impairs your bankruptcy exemptions. In Tennessee, this is commonly used to remove judgment liens from homes (homestead exemption: $5,000 (individual) / $7,500 (joint)) and vehicles (exemption: $10,000).

Does the judgment creditor have to stop collecting?

Yes. The automatic stay stops all collection on judgments the instant you file. The creditor cannot garnish wages, levy bank accounts, or enforce the judgment in any way while the stay is in effect.

Facing a Judgment?

Find out if bankruptcy can eliminate your judgment debt.

Discharge Screener
Tennessee Exemptions

## Chapter 13 Bankruptcy in Knoxville - Tennessee
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/knoxville/chapter-13.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsFAQMemphisNashvilleKnoxvilleChattanoogaOBP

Home›Knoxville›Chapter 13

Chapter 13 Bankruptcy in Knoxville

Chapter 13 lets you keep property while repaying debts over 3-5 years.

How Chapter 13 Works

Propose a repayment plan over 3-5 years. Keep your home and car while catching up on arrears. No means test.

Save your home from foreclosure

Keep your car and cure defaults

Stop wage garnishment via automatic stay

Filing fee: $313

Tennessee Non-Judicial Foreclosure

Tennessee is a non-judicial foreclosure state. Chapter 13's automatic stay is the most effective tool to stop foreclosure in Knoxville.

How Chapter 13 Works for Knoxville Filers

Chapter 13 is a court-supervised repayment plan, typically running three to five years. It is the right tool when you have regular income, want to catch up on a mortgage arrearage, protect a non-exempt asset, or failed the Chapter 7 means test. In Knoxville, Chapter 13 plans are administered by the standing trustee for your division, who collects your monthly payment and distributes it to creditors according to the confirmed plan.

The Chapter 13 process starts the same way as Chapter 7: credit counseling, petition, schedules, and the automatic stay. The key differences begin at filing. Along with your schedules, you file a proposed plan showing how much you will pay the trustee each month, for how many months, and how that money will be distributed. Your first plan payment is due within 30 days of filing - even before the plan is confirmed by the judge - and missing it is the single most common reason Chapter 13 cases are dismissed early.

Your plan payment is calculated by taking your monthly disposable income (from Form 122C) and applying a few rules. Above-median debtors must commit all disposable income for 60 months. Below-median debtors must commit all disposable income for 36 months. Secured debts being cured in the plan - a mortgage arrearage, a car loan being crammed down - are added on top. Priority debts like recent taxes and child support arrearage must be paid in full through the plan. General unsecured creditors get whatever is left after all of that.

When Chapter 13 Makes Sense

Mortgage arrearage: Chapter 13 lets you cure a foreclosure arrearage over up to 60 months while making current payments outside the plan.

Underwater car loans over 910 days old: You can "cram down" the loan to the car's actual value and pay the rest as unsecured.

Non-dischargeable tax debt: Priority taxes can be paid over 60 months with the automatic stay in place, stopping IRS collection.

Above-median income: If you failed the Chapter 7 means test, Chapter 13 is the fallback and always available.

Keeping non-exempt assets: If you have equity above your exemption limits, Chapter 13 lets you keep the asset in exchange for paying the equity value to unsecured creditors through the plan.

About half of all Chapter 13 cases nationally fail to reach discharge. The most common reasons are missed plan payments and post-filing income disruptions. Before filing, make sure the plan payment is actually affordable given your real monthly budget - not an optimistic budget built to squeeze past the confirmation hearing.

Frequently Asked Questions

How long does a Knoxville Chapter 13 plan last?

Three years if you are below the state median income, five years if you are above. The judge cannot confirm a plan shorter than three years unless it pays 100 percent of unsecured claims.

Can I pay off my Knoxville Chapter 13 plan early?

Only by paying 100 percent of unsecured claims. If you try to pay off a plan early with less than 100 percent to unsecureds, the trustee and unsecured creditors can object - and courts generally sustain those objections because the Bankruptcy Code requires committing all projected disposable income for the full commitment period.

What happens if I miss a Chapter 13 payment in Knoxville?

The trustee will file a motion to dismiss after one or two missed payments. You have a chance to cure the default by catching up, modifying the plan to add the missed amount to future payments, or converting to Chapter 7. Do not ignore a motion to dismiss - dismissal without a discharge means all your creditors come back.

Related resources from the Bankruptcy Transparency Network: means test, Chapter 7 vs. 13, 1328(f) prior-discharge screener, 341 meeting, automatic stay, how to file bankruptcy, student loans, Open Bankruptcy Project.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

Free Discharge Screener

Open Bankruptcy Project Network

OBP Home
1328(f) Screener
Ch. 7 vs 13
Automatic Stay
341 Meeting
Means Test
Cost Guide
Dismissed?
File Again?
How to File

Free, open-source bankruptcy transparency. No ads. No affiliate links. Supported by donations.

An Open Bankruptcy Project resource

Data sourced from the Federal Judicial Center Integrated Database (2008--2024).

This site provides general information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for your specific situation.

© 2026 Open Bankruptcy Project. GitHub

## Chapter 7 Bankruptcy in Knoxville - Tennessee
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/knoxville/chapter-7.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsFAQMemphisNashvilleKnoxvilleChattanoogaOBP

Home›Knoxville›Chapter 7

Chapter 7 Bankruptcy in Knoxville

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts in 3-4 months. Here is what Knoxville residents need to know.

What Is Chapter 7?

Chapter 7 eliminates credit cards, medical bills, and personal loans. Most Knoxville filers keep all property within exemption limits.

Knoxville Means Test

HouseholdAnnual Median

1$53,697

2$67,854

3$77,379

4$94,416

Timeline

1Credit counseling

2File at E.D. Tenn., 800 Market St, Suite 330, Knoxville, TN 37902

3341 meeting (~30 days)

4Financial management course

5Discharge (3-4 months)

Your Chapter 7 Case in Knoxville - What to Expect

Chapter 7 is the fastest path out of unmanageable debt. In Knoxville, most Chapter 7 cases close 90 to 120 days from the filing date, and the vast majority are 'no asset' cases where the debtor keeps everything they own. The automatic stay starts the moment you file, which means creditors cannot call, sue, garnish, or repossess while your case is pending. Here is what the process actually looks like for Knoxville filers.

Before you file, you complete a pre-filing credit counseling course from an approved provider - usually online, about 60 minutes, under $25. You also gather your documents: the last two years of tax returns, the last six months of pay stubs, three months of bank statements, titles to your vehicles, your mortgage statement if you own a home, and a list of every creditor. Your petition, schedules, and statement of financial affairs are then filed electronically with the bankruptcy court. Filing opens a new case number and triggers the automatic stay.

About 20 to 40 days after filing, you attend the section 341 meeting of creditors. In Tennessee, most 341 meetings are now conducted by Zoom or phone rather than in person. You are placed under oath and questioned by the Chapter 7 trustee about your schedules. Typical questions: Did you list all your assets? Have you transferred anything valuable in the last two years? Do you expect a tax refund or inheritance? The meeting usually lasts five to ten minutes. Creditors rarely attend.

After the 341, the trustee investigates whether any of your assets can be sold for the benefit of creditors. In the great majority of Knoxville Chapter 7 cases, the answer is no - your exempt property is protected, and the trustee files a "no distribution" report. You complete a second class (a post-filing financial management course) and wait. Roughly 60 days after the 341 meeting, the court issues your discharge order, wiping out the dischargeable debts and giving you a fresh start.

What Chapter 7 Does Not Wipe Out

Student loans in most cases (requires adversary proceeding for hardship discharge)

Recent tax debts (generally anything less than three years old)

Child support and alimony

Criminal fines and restitution

Debts obtained by fraud or false pretenses

Debts not listed on your schedules

Frequently Asked Questions

How long does a Knoxville Chapter 7 take from filing to discharge?

Most cases discharge 90 to 120 days after filing. The minimum statutory period is 60 days after the 341 meeting. Cases with asset sales or objections take longer - sometimes a year or more.

Can I keep my car in a Knoxville Chapter 7 case?

Usually yes, if you are current on the loan and your equity is within your exemption limits. You can either 'reaffirm' the debt (sign a new agreement to keep paying), 'redeem' it (pay the current value in one lump sum), or let it go. Most filers reaffirm.

How much does a Chapter 7 attorney cost in Knoxville?

Attorney fees vary, but the typical range for a straightforward consumer Chapter 7 in most districts runs from about $1,000 to $2,500 plus the $338 court filing fee. Be careful with firms advertising '$0 down' Chapter 7 - upfront attorney fees in Chapter 7 are discharged if unpaid at filing, so 'no money down' is usually a Chapter 13 pitch.

Related resources from the Bankruptcy Transparency Network: means test, Chapter 7 vs. 13, 1328(f) prior-discharge screener, 341 meeting, automatic stay, how to file bankruptcy, student loans, Open Bankruptcy Project.

## Knoxville Bankruptcy Exemptions - State vs Federal - Tennessee
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/knoxville/exemptions.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsFAQMemphisNashvilleKnoxvilleChattanoogaOBP

Home›Knoxville›Exemptions

Knoxville Bankruptcy Exemptions - State vs Federal

Tennessee lets you choose state or federal exemptions. Compare to protect the most property.

Exemption Comparison

PropertyTennessee ExemptionFederal Alternative

Homestead$5,000 individual / $7,500 family$27,900

Vehicle$10,000 (wildcard)$4,450

Household goods$4,000 total$700 per item / $14,875 total

Wildcard$10,000$1,475 + up to $13,950 unused homestead

Retirement (IRA/401k)Fully exemptFully exempt

Social SecurityFully exemptFully exempt

For homeowners: federal ($27,900) beats state ($5,000/$7,500). For renters with personal property: state's $10,000 wildcard may be better.

Tennessee Exemptions Explained for Knoxville Filers

Exemptions are the rules that decide what you keep when you file bankruptcy. Everything you own becomes part of the bankruptcy estate the moment you file, and exemptions are the legal carve-outs that pull property back out of the estate and into your hands. For Knoxville residents, the exemption question has three parts: which set of exemptions you can use, how much each exemption protects, and what happens to property that exceeds the limits.

State exemption law governs in most Tennessee bankruptcy cases. The federal exemptions in 11 U.S.C. section 522(d) are available only in "opt-in" states; in opt-out states, filers must use the state set. Check your state's current opt-in status before relying on federal numbers - some states allow a choice, others do not. In either case, you cannot mix and match: you pick one entire set of exemptions, not the best items from each.

The biggest exemption for most filers is the homestead - the equity in your primary residence you can protect from creditors. Homestead amounts vary wildly by state, from a few thousand dollars in some states to unlimited in others (Florida, Texas, Kansas, Iowa, Oklahoma, South Dakota). Domicile rules matter here: if you moved to your current state within the last 730 days, you may have to use the exemptions of your previous state under 11 U.S.C. section 522(b)(3)(A). Plan the filing timing accordingly.

Common Exemption Categories

Homestead: Equity in your primary residence.

Motor vehicle: Equity in one or more vehicles, usually a few thousand dollars per vehicle.

Personal property: Household goods, clothing, appliances, up to a cap.

Tools of the trade: Equipment you need to earn a living.

Retirement accounts: 401(k), pension, and IRA balances are almost always fully protected.

Wildcard: A dollar amount you can apply to any property, including cash.

Life insurance cash value: Usually protected up to a cap.

Public benefits: Social Security, unemployment, workers compensation, veterans benefits.

If you own property that exceeds your exemption limit - say, a paid-off car worth $12,000 when your exemption protects only $4,000 - the Chapter 7 trustee has the right to sell it, pay you the exempt amount, and distribute the rest to your creditors. In practice, trustees often negotiate with the debtor to buy the non-exempt portion rather than actually liquidate. Alternatively, you can file Chapter 13 and pay the non-exempt value to unsecureds through your plan instead of surrendering the asset.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I use the federal bankruptcy exemptions instead of Tennessee?

It depends on whether Tennessee has opted out of the federal exemption scheme. Most states have opted out, meaning you must use the state exemptions. A handful of states (and DC) allow filers to choose. Confirm current opt-in status before you file - it changes how you fill out Schedule C.

What if I moved to this state less than two years ago?

Under 11 U.S.C. section 522(b)(3)(A), if you have not been domiciled in your current state for the 730 days before filing, you use the exemptions of the state where you lived the greater part of the 180 days before that 730-day window. If that earlier state only allows its exemptions for residents, you may be forced into the federal set as a fallback.

Are retirement accounts safe in bankruptcy?

Yes, almost always. ERISA-qualified plans (401(k), 403(b), pension) are excluded from the estate entirely. Traditional and Roth IRAs are exempt up to a federal cap that is adjusted periodically - currently over $1.5 million in aggregate. Inherited IRAs are treated differently and may not receive the same protection under Clark v. Rameker (2014).

Related resources from the Bankruptcy Transparency Network: means test, Chapter 7 vs. 13, 1328(f) prior-discharge screener, 341 meeting, automatic stay, how to file bankruptcy, student loans, Open Bankruptcy Project.

## Knoxville Bankruptcy FAQ - Tennessee
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/knoxville/faq.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsFAQMemphisNashvilleKnoxvilleChattanoogaOBP

Home›Knoxville›FAQ

Knoxville Bankruptcy FAQ

Answers to common questions about filing bankruptcy in Knoxville.

Where do I file in Knoxville?

Eastern District of Tennessee, 800 Market St, Suite 330, Knoxville, TN 37902. Phone: (865) 545-4279.

How much does bankruptcy cost?

Chapter 7: $1,238-$2,188. Chapter 13: $2,838-$4,363.

State or federal exemptions?

Tennessee lets you choose. Federal homestead ($27,900) is usually better for homeowners. Details.

How long does bankruptcy take?

Chapter 7: 3-4 months. Chapter 13: 3-5 years.

What is the 341 meeting?

Brief hearing with the trustee ~30 days after filing. Learn more.

Can I file again?

Yes, with timing rules. Check eligibility.

Bankruptcy in Knoxville, Tennessee

Knoxville residents file in the Eastern District of Tennessee. This free guide covers Chapter 7, Chapter 13, Tennessee exemptions, costs, and what to expect when filing in East Tennessee.

Read the FAQ

TN Homestead Exemption

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

Chapter 7 - Fresh Start

Eliminates most unsecured debts in 3-4 months. Must pass the means test. Filing fee: $338.

Keep property, repay over 3-5 years. No means test. Filing fee: $313.

Knoxville Courthouse

Eastern District of Tennessee - Knoxville Division

Courthouse: 800 Market St, Suite 330, Knoxville, TN 37902

Phone: (865) 545-4279 | tneb.uscourts.gov

The Eastern District also covers Chattanooga (separate division). Knoxville residents file in the Knoxville division.

## Filing Bankruptcy for Medical Debt in Tennessee - Discharge, Alternatives, Protections
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/medical-debt-only.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostGlossaryFaqOBP

Home›Filing Bankruptcy for Medical Debt in Tennessee

Filing Bankruptcy for Medical Debt in Tennessee

Medical debt is the leading cause of bankruptcy in America. It is fully dischargeable in both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13. You do not need to feel guilty about discharging medical debt - the system is designed for exactly this situation.

Quick answer: Medical debt is 100% dischargeable in bankruptcy. You cannot file only for medical debt (all eligible debts are included), but medical bills are unsecured, non-priority debt - the easiest category to discharge. Before filing, check if you qualify for hospital charity care or financial assistance programs in Tennessee.

Medical Debt in Bankruptcy: The Basics

Medical debt is classified as general unsecured, non-priority debt in bankruptcy - the same category as credit cards and personal loans. This is the most favorable classification for discharge:

Chapter 7: Medical debt is completely eliminated when you receive your discharge (typically 3-4 months after filing)

Chapter 13: Medical debt is paid at whatever percentage your plan pays to unsecured creditors. In many Chapter 13 plans, unsecured creditors receive 10% or less. A $50,000 medical bill might result in paying $5,000 or less over 3-5 years.

No exception to discharge: Unlike student loans or certain tax debts, there is no exception that prevents medical debt from being discharged

Tennessee Medical Debt Protections

Medical debt is fully dischargeable in Tennessee bankruptcy. Tennessee did not expand Medicaid under the ACA for most of the period (a block-grant waiver was recently approved), leading to a significant coverage gap that increased medical debt. Tennessee does not have specific medical debt protection statutes. Tennessee hospitals receiving Hill-Burton funds must provide charity care. Medical debt is a leading driver of bankruptcy in Tennessee.

Before Filing: Exhaust Other Options

Hospital Financial Assistance (Charity Care)

Under the ACA, nonprofit hospitals are required to have financial assistance policies. Many hospitals will significantly reduce or eliminate bills for patients below certain income thresholds (typically 200-400% of the federal poverty level). Ask the billing department about financial assistance before paying or filing bankruptcy.

Negotiate the Bill

Medical bills are often negotiable. Strategies include:

Request an itemized bill and dispute any errors

Ask for the "self-pay" or "uninsured" rate (often 40-60% less than the billed amount)

Offer a lump-sum settlement for less than the full amount

Request a payment plan with no interest

When Bankruptcy Makes More Sense

Bankruptcy is often the better choice when:

Medical debt is combined with other unsecured debts (credit cards, personal loans)

Total debt exceeds your ability to repay within a reasonable time

Creditors are suing or garnishing wages

The hospital denies charity care or the remaining balance is still unaffordable

Medical debt is on top of existing financial stress

Credit Report Changes for Medical Debt

Recent changes to credit reporting have reduced the impact of medical debt:

Medical debt under $500: No longer reported by Equifax, Experian, or TransUnion (as of 2023)

Paid medical collections: Removed from credit reports

1-year waiting period: Medical debt cannot appear on credit reports until at least 1 year after going to collections

FICO 10T and VantageScore 4.0: Newer scoring models reduce the weight of medical collections

Despite these improvements, large medical debts sent to third-party collectors can still damage your credit. Bankruptcy provides a definitive resolution.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I file bankruptcy just for medical debt?

You cannot selectively discharge only medical debt - all eligible unsecured debts are included. But if medical debt is your primary problem, bankruptcy eliminates it along with other unsecured debts.

Is medical debt dischargeable?

Yes, 100%. Medical debt is unsecured, non-priority debt - the easiest category to discharge. There is no exception preventing discharge.

Should I negotiate or file bankruptcy?

If medical debt is your only debt, try negotiation and charity care first. If you have multiple debts or the amounts are unmanageable, bankruptcy may be more efficient.

Will medical debt affect my credit?

Medical debt under $500 is no longer reported. Paid medical collections are removed. Large unpaid amounts can still affect credit until resolved through payment, settlement, or bankruptcy.

Does Tennessee have medical debt protections?

Protections vary. Check with your hospital about financial assistance programs. Nonprofit hospitals are required to have charity care policies under federal law.

Related Topics

Exemptions
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House
Credit After
Wage Garnishment
Medical Debt

## The 341 Meeting in Memphis - What to Expect - Tennessee
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/memphis/341-meeting.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsFAQMemphisNashvilleKnoxvilleChattanoogaOBP

Home›Memphis›341 Meeting

The 341 Meeting in Memphis - What to Expect

The 341 meeting of creditors is a brief hearing about 30 days after you file bankruptcy. Here is what Memphis filers need to know.

What Is the 341 Meeting?

The 341 meeting of creditors (named after 11 U.S.C. section 341) is a required hearing where you answer questions under oath about your finances, assets, and the information in your petition.

The meeting is conducted by the Chapter 7 or Chapter 13 trustee, not a judge. It typically lasts 5-10 minutes if everything is in order.

Where and When

Location: 341 meetings in Memphis are typically held at or near the W.D. Tenn. courthouse at 200 Jefferson Ave, Suite 413, Memphis, TN 38103.

Timing: Usually scheduled 20-40 days after filing. You will receive a notice with the exact date, time, and location.

What to Bring

Government-issued photo ID (driver's license or passport)

Social Security card or proof of SSN

Recent pay stubs (last 60 days)

Most recent tax return

Bank statements (last 2-3 months)

Common Questions Asked

Did you review the petition before signing?

Is the information accurate?

Do you own any real estate?

Have you transferred or sold any property recently?

Are you expecting any inheritance or lawsuit settlement?

Do you have any additional bank accounts?

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

## The Automatic Stay in Memphis Bankruptcy - Tennessee
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/memphis/automatic-stay.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsFAQMemphisNashvilleKnoxvilleChattanoogaOBP

Home›Memphis›Automatic Stay

The Automatic Stay in Memphis Bankruptcy

The moment you file bankruptcy, the automatic stay stops most collection actions against you. This is one of the most powerful protections in bankruptcy law.

What the Automatic Stay Stops

Stopped by Automatic Stay

Foreclosure proceedings

Vehicle repossession

Wage garnishment

Lawsuits and judgments

Collection calls and letters

Utility disconnection (20 days)

Bank account levies

NOT Stopped

Criminal proceedings

Child support / alimony actions

Tax audits (assessment only)

Eviction (in some cases)

Pension loan repayment

Tennessee-Specific Concerns

Tennessee uses non-judicial foreclosure, meaning your lender can foreclose without going through court. This makes the automatic stay especially critical for Memphis homeowners - it may be the only thing stopping a foreclosure sale.

However, be aware that repeat filers face reduced protection:

Second filing within 1 year: Automatic stay expires after 30 days unless you prove good faith

Third+ filing within 1 year: No automatic stay at all unless you get a court order

Given the 53.5% prior-filer rate in W.D. Tenn., many Memphis filers may face these limitations.

Learn more at automaticstay.org.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

Ch. 7 vs 13

## Chapter 13 Bankruptcy in Memphis - Repayment Plan Guide - Tennessee
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/memphis/chapter-13.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsFAQMemphisNashvilleKnoxvilleChattanoogaOBP

Home›Memphis›Chapter 13

Chapter 13 Bankruptcy in Memphis

Chapter 13 lets you keep your property while repaying debts over 3-5 years. But in Memphis, the numbers are alarming: W.D. Tenn. has the highest Chapter 13 dismissal rate in the entire country.

79.6% of Chapter 13 cases in W.D. Tenn. end in dismissal

79.6%

Dismissal Rate

53.5%

Prior Filer Rate

3-5 yrs

Plan Length

$313

Filing Fee

How Chapter 13 Works

In Chapter 13, you propose a repayment plan to pay back some or all of your debts over 3-5 years. You make monthly payments to a trustee, who distributes funds to creditors. At the end, remaining eligible unsecured debts are discharged.

Chapter 13 is especially useful for:

Saving your home - catch up on missed mortgage payments over the plan

Keeping your car - cure defaults and potentially reduce the loan balance

Stopping wage garnishments - the automatic stay halts collections

Repaying tax debts - spread tax obligations over 3-5 years

The Memphis Dismissal Problem

W.D. Tenn. has a 79.6% Chapter 13 dismissal rate, making it the worst-performing district in the country. This means fewer than 1 in 4 Chapter 13 filers here actually receive a discharge.

The 53.5% prior-filer rate indicates that many people in this district file Chapter 13 multiple times without ever completing a plan. Each filing costs money and time while providing only temporary relief from the automatic stay.

Before filing Chapter 13 in Memphis, seriously consider whether Chapter 7 is an option. If you qualify for Chapter 7, it eliminates debt in months rather than years and does not depend on completing a multi-year plan.

Chapter 13 Timeline

1Credit counseling - Required before filing

2File petition + plan - Submit to W.D. Tenn.

3Begin payments - Within 30 days of filing (before confirmation)

4341 meeting - 20-50 days after filing

5Confirmation hearing - Court approves the plan

6Complete plan - 3-5 years of payments

7Financial management course + discharge

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the Chapter 13 dismissal rate in Memphis?

79.6% - the worst in the nation. More than 3 out of 4 Chapter 13 cases in the Western District of Tennessee end in dismissal rather than discharge.

Can I save my house from foreclosure in Chapter 13?

Yes. The automatic stay stops foreclosure immediately, and your plan can include catch-up payments on the mortgage arrearage. More details.

What happens if my Chapter 13 is dismissed?

Dismissal means creditors can resume collection activity. You lose the protection of the automatic stay. Repeated filings may result in a shortened or denied stay. Learn more about dismissal.

Should I file Chapter 7 instead?

Given the 79.6% dismissal rate, Chapter 7 may be the safer option if you qualify. Compare Chapter 7 vs 13.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

Free Discharge Screener

Open Bankruptcy Project Network

Ch. 7 vs 13
