## Chapter 7 Bankruptcy in Memphis - Eligibility, Exemptions, Timeline - Tennessee
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/memphis/chapter-7.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsFAQMemphisNashvilleKnoxvilleChattanoogaOBP

Home›Memphis›Chapter 7

Chapter 7 Bankruptcy in Memphis

Chapter 7 is the fastest path to debt relief. Most unsecured debts are eliminated in 3-4 months. Here is what Memphis residents need to know.

What Is Chapter 7?

Chapter 7, sometimes called "liquidation bankruptcy" or a "fresh start," eliminates most unsecured debts. A court-appointed trustee reviews your assets. If you have non-exempt property, the trustee may sell it to pay creditors - but most Chapter 7 cases are "no-asset" cases where the filer keeps everything.

In Memphis, the vast majority of Chapter 7 filers keep all their property because it falls within Tennessee exemption limits or the more generous federal exemptions.

Debts Chapter 7 Eliminates

Dischargeable

Credit card debt

Medical bills

Personal loans

Utility bills

Some older tax debts

Deficiency balances

NOT Dischargeable

Student loans (usually)

Recent taxes (last 3 years)

Child support / alimony

Debts from fraud

Criminal fines

Most government debts

Memphis Means Test

The means test compares your household income to the Tennessee state median. If your income is below the median, you automatically qualify for Chapter 7.

2026 Tennessee Median Income Thresholds

Household SizeAnnual MedianMonthly

1 person$53,697$4,475

2 persons$67,854$5,655

3 persons$77,379$6,448

4 persons$94,416$7,868

If your income exceeds the median, you may still qualify after deducting allowed expenses. Full means test guide →

Chapter 7 Timeline in Memphis

1Credit counseling - Complete an approved course (within 180 days before filing)

2File petition - Submit forms to W.D. Tenn. at 200 Jefferson Ave, Suite 413, Memphis, TN 38103

3341 meeting - About 30 days after filing. Answer questions under oath.

4Financial management course - Complete before discharge

5Discharge - Typically 60-90 days after 341 meeting (3-4 months total)

Tennessee Exemptions in Chapter 7

Tennessee allows you to choose between state and federal exemptions. This is a significant advantage - compare both to see which protects more of your property.

PropertyTennessee ExemptionFederal Alternative

Homestead$5,000 individual / $7,500 family$27,900

Vehicle$10,000 (wildcard)$4,450

Household goods$4,000 total$700 per item / $14,875 total

Wildcard$10,000$1,475 + up to $13,950 unused homestead

Retirement (IRA/401k)Fully exemptFully exempt

Social SecurityFully exemptFully exempt

Full exemption comparison →

Frequently Asked Questions

What debts does Chapter 7 eliminate?

Chapter 7 discharges most unsecured debts including credit cards, medical bills, personal loans, and old utility bills. It does not discharge student loans (usually), recent taxes, child support, alimony, or debts from fraud.

Will I lose my car in Chapter 7?

Tennessee's $10,000 wildcard exemption can protect vehicle equity. Alternatively, the federal vehicle exemption is $4,450. If you owe more than the car is worth, you can usually keep it by reaffirming the loan. Learn more.

How long does Chapter 7 take in Memphis?

From filing to discharge, about 3-4 months. The 341 meeting is about 30 days after filing.

Can I file Chapter 7 if I have a job?

Yes. Having a job does not disqualify you. The means test compares your income to Tennessee's median. Many working people qualify. Check the means test.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

## Memphis Bankruptcy Filing Checklist - Tennessee
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/memphis/checklist.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsFAQMemphisNashvilleKnoxvilleChattanoogaOBP

Home›Memphis›Checklist

Memphis Bankruptcy Filing Checklist

Use this checklist to prepare for your Memphis bankruptcy filing. Proper preparation helps ensure a smooth process.

Documents to Gather

Last 6 months of pay stubs or income records

Last 2 years of federal and state tax returns

Bank statements (all accounts, last 3-6 months)

Mortgage statements and property tax bills

Vehicle titles and loan statements

Credit card and loan statements (all debts)

Medical bills and collection letters

Insurance policies

Retirement account statements (401k, IRA)

Any court judgments, garnishment orders, or lawsuits

Lease agreements

Government-issued photo ID

Social Security card

Steps Before Filing

1Complete credit counseling - From an approved provider (within 180 days of filing)

2Decide: state or federal exemptions - Compare to protect the most property. Exemption guide

3Run the means test - Determine Chapter 7 eligibility. Means test guide

4List ALL debts - Missing a debt means it may not be discharged

5List ALL assets - Hiding assets is a federal crime

6Stop using credit cards - Charges near filing time may be considered fraud

7Do not transfer property - Transfers within 2 years can be reversed

Common Mistakes to Avoid

Paying back family members before filing (preferential transfers)

Running up credit card debt before filing

Transferring property to friends or family

Cashing out retirement accounts (they are exempt!)

Filing without comparing state vs. federal exemptions

Not listing all creditors and debts

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

## How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Memphis? - Tennessee
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/memphis/cost.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsFAQMemphisNashvilleKnoxvilleChattanoogaOBP

Home›Memphis›Cost

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Memphis?

A complete breakdown of bankruptcy costs for Memphis residents, including filing fees, attorney fees, and ways to reduce your out-of-pocket expenses.

Cost Breakdown

ExpenseChapter 7Chapter 13

Court filing fee$338$313

Attorney fees (typical)$900 - $1,800$2,500 - $4,000

Credit counseling (2 courses)$25 - $50$25 - $50

Total (typical)$1,263 - $2,188$2,838 - $4,363

Ways to Reduce Costs

Fee waiver (Chapter 7): If your income is below 150% of federal poverty guidelines

Installment payments: Ask the court to pay the filing fee in installments

Chapter 13 fees through plan: Attorney fees can be paid through your repayment plan

Pro se filing: You can file without an attorney, though this is not recommended

Legal aid: Contact Memphis Area Legal Services at (901) 523-8822

This site provides general educational information about bankruptcy law, not legal advice. Every situation is different. Consult with a qualified bankruptcy attorney licensed in Tennessee before making any decisions about filing.

## Rebuilding Credit After Bankruptcy in Memphis - Tennessee
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/memphis/credit-after-bankruptcy.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsFAQMemphisNashvilleKnoxvilleChattanoogaOBP

Home›Memphis›Credit After Bankruptcy

Rebuilding Credit After Bankruptcy in Memphis

Bankruptcy is not the end of your financial life - it is a fresh start. Here is how to rebuild your credit after filing in Memphis.

Credit Report Timeline

EventDuration on Credit Report

Chapter 7 filing10 years from filing date

Chapter 13 filing7 years from filing date

Individual discharged debts7 years from date of default

Steps to Rebuild

1Check your credit reports - Ensure all discharged debts show $0 balance. Dispute errors.

2Get a secured credit card - Start with a small deposit ($200-$500). Use it lightly and pay in full monthly.

3Pay all current bills on time - Payment history is the biggest factor in your credit score.

4Keep credit utilization low - Use less than 30% of available credit (ideally under 10%).

5Consider a credit-builder loan - Available through credit unions and some banks.

6Be patient - Most filers see significant credit improvement within 1-2 years.

Realistic Expectations

6 months after: You may qualify for secured credit cards

1-2 years after: Many filers reach 650+ credit scores

2-3 years after: You may qualify for a car loan at reasonable rates

3-4 years after: FHA mortgage may be possible (Chapter 7 requires 2-year wait)

Learn more at rebuildcreditafterbankruptcy.com.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

## Memphis Bankruptcy Exemptions - State vs Federal - Tennessee
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/memphis/exemptions.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsFAQMemphisNashvilleKnoxvilleChattanoogaOBP

Home›Memphis›Exemptions

Memphis Bankruptcy Exemptions

Tennessee allows you to choose between state and federal exemptions - a significant advantage. Compare both systems to protect the most property.

State vs. Federal Exemptions

Tennessee is one of the states that lets filers choose between state exemptions and the federal bankruptcy exemptions under 11 U.S.C. section 522(d). You must pick one system - you cannot mix and match.

For many Memphis filers, federal exemptions are the better choice because the federal homestead exemption ($27,900) is much higher than Tennessee's ($5,000/$7,500).

PropertyTennessee ExemptionFederal Alternative

Homestead$5,000 individual / $7,500 family$27,900

Vehicle$10,000 (wildcard)$4,450

Household goods$4,000 total$700 per item / $14,875 total

Wildcard$10,000$1,475 + up to $13,950 unused homestead

Retirement (IRA/401k)Fully exemptFully exempt

Social SecurityFully exemptFully exempt

When to Choose State Exemptions

You have little or no home equity but significant personal property

Tennessee's $10,000 wildcard covers your most valuable assets

You have large household goods collections (state: $4,000)

When to Choose Federal Exemptions

You own a home with equity between $7,500 and $27,900

You need the federal vehicle exemption ($4,450)

You are not married (state homestead is only $5,000 for individuals)

You want to stack the unused homestead as wildcard ($1,475 + up to $13,950)

Frequently Asked Questions

Can married couples double exemptions in Tennessee?

If both spouses file jointly, each spouse claims their own set of exemptions. Both must choose the same system (both state or both federal).

What happens to property that is not exempt?

In Chapter 7, the trustee may sell non-exempt property to pay creditors. In Chapter 13, non-exempt property value determines your minimum plan payment but you keep everything.

Is my retirement account protected?

Yes. 401(k)s, IRAs, and other qualified retirement accounts are fully exempt under both state and federal exemption systems.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

Free Discharge Screener

Open Bankruptcy Project Network

OBP Home
1328(f) Screener
Ch. 7 vs 13
Automatic Stay
341 Meeting
Means Test
Cost Guide
Dismissed?
File Again?

## Memphis Bankruptcy FAQ - Common Questions Answered - Tennessee
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/memphis/faq.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsFAQMemphisNashvilleKnoxvilleChattanoogaOBP

Home›Memphis›FAQ

Memphis Bankruptcy FAQ

Answers to the most common questions about filing bankruptcy in Memphis, Tennessee.

General Questions

How much does bankruptcy cost in Memphis?

Chapter 7: $338 filing fee + $900-$1,800 attorney fees. Chapter 13: $313 filing fee + $2,500-$4,000 attorney fees. Credit counseling adds $25-$50. Full breakdown.

What is the automatic stay?

The automatic stay is a court order that immediately stops foreclosure, repossession, wage garnishment, lawsuits, and most other collection actions the moment you file.

Where do I file for bankruptcy in Memphis?

Western District of Tennessee, 200 Jefferson Ave, Suite 413, Memphis, TN 38103. Phone: (901) 328-3500.

Should I file Chapter 7 or Chapter 13?

Chapter 7 eliminates debt in 3-4 months but requires passing a means test. Chapter 13 lets you keep property through a 3-5 year plan but has a 79.6% dismissal rate in W.D. Tenn. Compare chapters.

Can I file bankruptcy without an attorney?

Yes, you can file "pro se," but it is not recommended. Bankruptcy involves complex forms and procedures. Mistakes can lead to dismissal or loss of property.

What is the means test?

The means test determines Chapter 7 eligibility by comparing your income to Tennessee's median. Memphis means test guide.

How long does bankruptcy stay on my credit?

Chapter 7: 10 years from filing. Chapter 13: 7 years from filing. Rebuilding your credit.

Will I lose my house?

It depends on your equity and which exemption system you choose. Tennessee allows federal exemptions ($27,900 homestead). Full guide.

Will I lose my car?

Usually not. Tennessee's $10,000 wildcard or federal $4,450 vehicle exemption protects most vehicles. Full guide.

What is the 341 meeting?

The 341 meeting of creditors is a brief hearing about 30 days after filing where you answer questions under oath about your finances. What to expect.

Can I file bankruptcy again?

Yes, but timing rules apply. Chapter 7 to Chapter 7: 8 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 13: 2 years. Full timing rules or use the 1328(f) screener.

Why is the dismissal rate so high in Memphis?

W.D. Tenn. has a 79.6% Chapter 13 dismissal rate. Contributing factors include poverty, aggressive filing practices, and a 53.5% prior-filer rate suggesting repeat filings without plan completion.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

Free Discharge Screener

Open Bankruptcy Project Network

OBP Home
1328(f) Screener
Ch. 7 vs 13
Automatic Stay
341 Meeting
Means Test
Cost Guide
Dismissed?
File Again?
How to File

Free, open-source bankruptcy transparency. No ads. No affiliate links. Supported by donations.

An Open Bankruptcy Project resource

## Bankruptcy in Memphis, Tennessee - Complete Guide
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/memphis/

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsFAQMemphisNashvilleKnoxvilleChattanoogaOBP

Bankruptcy in Memphis, Tennessee

Memphis residents file in the Western District of Tennessee. With a staggering 79.6% Chapter 13 dismissal rate - the worst in the nation - and a 53.5% prior filer rate, understanding your options before filing is critical.

W.D. Tenn. Chapter 13 Dismissal Rate: 79.6% - Worst in the U.S.

Read the FAQ
Check Your Eligibility

79.6%

Ch. 13 Dismissal Rate

53.5%

Prior Filer Rate

$5,000 / $7,500

TN Homestead Exemption

$338

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13: Which Is Right for You?

Chapter 7 - Fresh Start

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts (credit cards, medical bills, personal loans) in about 3-4 months. You must pass a means test based on your income.

Discharge in 3-4 months

Eliminates credit cards, medical debt

Must pass the means test

May lose non-exempt property

Filing fee: $338

Learn more about Chapter 7 →

Chapter 13 - Payment Plan

Chapter 13 lets you keep your property while repaying debts over 3-5 years. Good for saving a home from foreclosure or a car from repossession.

3-5 year repayment plan

Keep your home and car

Catch up on missed payments

No means test requirement

Filing fee: $313

Learn more about Chapter 13 →

Warning: W.D. Tenn. has the highest Chapter 13 dismissal rate in the nation at 79.6%. More than 3 out of 4 Chapter 13 cases here end in dismissal. Consider Chapter 7 if you qualify, or research local attorney success rates carefully before filing Chapter 13.

Not sure which chapter fits? Compare them in detail.

Memphis Courthouse

Western District of Tennessee - Memphis Division

Courthouse: 200 Jefferson Ave, Suite 413, Memphis, TN 38103

Phone: (901) 328-3500 | tnwb.uscourts.gov

All bankruptcy filings for Memphis and western Tennessee residents are handled here. Tennessee uses a non-judicial foreclosure process, meaning your home can be foreclosed without a court proceeding - which makes the automatic stay especially important.

Tennessee Exemptions at a Glance

Tennessee filers can choose between state and federal exemptions. For Memphis homeowners with significant equity, federal exemptions are often the better choice because the federal homestead exemption ($27,900) far exceeds Tennessee's ($5,000/$7,500).

PropertyTennessee ExemptionFederal Alternative

Homestead$5,000 individual / $7,500 family$27,900

Vehicle$10,000 (wildcard)$4,450

Household goods$4,000 total$700 per item / $14,875 total

Wildcard$10,000$1,475 + up to $13,950 unused homestead

Retirement (IRA/401k)Fully exemptFully exempt

Social SecurityFully exemptFully exempt

Full exemption details →

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Memphis?

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338 (can request installment payments)

Chapter 13 filing fee: $313 (can be paid through your plan)

Attorney fees: Typically $900-$1,800 for Chapter 7; $2,500-$4,000 for Chapter 13

Credit counseling: $15-25 per course (two required)

Fee waivers are available for Chapter 7 filers whose income is below 150% of the federal poverty guidelines.

Full cost breakdown → | National cost guide

Free Legal Resources in Memphis

Memphis Area Legal Services

Phone: (901) 523-8822 | Provides free legal assistance to low-income Memphis residents, including bankruptcy guidance.

Tennessee also has a Lawyer Referral Service through the Tennessee Bar Association.

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does bankruptcy cost in Memphis?

Chapter 7 filing fees are $338 and Chapter 13 filing fees are $313. Attorney fees typically range from $900-$1,800 for Chapter 7 and $2,500-$4,000 for Chapter 13. Full cost breakdown.

Will I lose my house filing bankruptcy in Memphis?

Tennessee's homestead exemption is $5,000/$7,500, but you can choose federal exemptions ($27,900 homestead) instead. In Chapter 13, you can keep your home while catching up on payments. Learn more.

Why is the dismissal rate so high in Memphis?

W.D. Tenn. has a 79.6% Chapter 13 dismissal rate - the worst nationally. Contributing factors include high poverty rates, aggressive filing practices, and the 53.5% prior-filer rate suggesting many people are filing repeatedly without success.

Where do I file for bankruptcy in Memphis?

Memphis residents file in the Western District of Tennessee at 200 Jefferson Ave, Suite 413, Memphis, TN 38103. Phone: (901) 328-3500.

See all frequently asked questions →

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

## Can You Keep Your Car in Memphis Bankruptcy? - Tennessee
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/memphis/keep-your-car.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsFAQMemphisNashvilleKnoxvilleChattanoogaOBP

Home›Memphis›Keep Your Car

Can You Keep Your Car in Memphis Bankruptcy?

Most Memphis residents can keep their car when filing bankruptcy. Here is how to protect your vehicle using Tennessee or federal exemptions.

Vehicle Exemptions

SystemVehicle Protection

Tennessee state$10,000 wildcard (no specific vehicle exemption)

Federal$4,450 vehicle + $1,475 wildcard (+ up to $13,950 unused homestead)

If you do not own a home, the federal wildcard can protect up to $19,875 in vehicle equity ($4,450 + $1,475 + $13,950).

Options for Cars with Loans

Reaffirmation: Sign a new agreement to keep paying. Keeps the car but you remain liable.

Redemption: Pay the car's current value in a lump sum, discharge the remaining balance.

Surrender: Return the car and discharge the remaining debt.

Chapter 13 cramdown: If the loan is over 910 days old, you may reduce the balance to the car's current value.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

## Can You Keep Your House in Memphis Bankruptcy? - Tennessee
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/memphis/keep-your-house.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsFAQMemphisNashvilleKnoxvilleChattanoogaOBP

Home›Memphis›Keep Your House

Can You Keep Your House in Memphis Bankruptcy?

Tennessee has a low state homestead exemption, but you can choose federal exemptions - which protect far more equity. Here is how to keep your home.

Homestead Exemption Comparison

SystemHomestead Protection

Tennessee state (individual)$5,000

Tennessee state (family/62+)$7,500

Federal$27,900

Federal exemptions protect over 5x more home equity than Tennessee state exemptions. Most Memphis homeowners should choose federal.

Non-Judicial Foreclosure Warning

Tennessee is a non-judicial foreclosure state. Your lender can foreclose without going to court, with as little as 20 days' notice. The bankruptcy automatic stay is the most effective way to stop a non-judicial foreclosure.

Chapter 7 vs Chapter 13 for Homeowners

Chapter 7

Keep home if equity is within exemptions

Must be current on mortgage or catch up quickly

Does not help with mortgage arrears

Chapter 13

Catch up on missed payments over 3-5 years

Strip off junior liens if underwater

But: 79.6% dismissal rate in W.D. Tenn.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

## Memphis Means Test - Do You Qualify for Chapter 7? - Tennessee
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/memphis/means-test.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsFAQMemphisNashvilleKnoxvilleChattanoogaOBP

Home›Memphis›Means Test

Memphis Means Test - Do You Qualify for Chapter 7?

The means test determines whether you can file Chapter 7 bankruptcy. It compares your income to the Tennessee state median for your household size.

2026 Tennessee Median Income Thresholds

Household SizeAnnual MedianMonthly

1 person$53,697$4,475

2 persons$67,854$5,655

3 persons$77,379$6,448

4 persons$94,416$7,868

Add $9,900 for each additional person beyond 4.

How the Means Test Works

1Calculate your income - Average your last 6 months of gross income

2Compare to median - If below Tennessee's median for your household size, you pass automatically

3If above median - Deduct allowed expenses (mortgage, car payments, taxes, health insurance, etc.)

4Calculate disposable income - If remaining disposable income is low enough, you still qualify

Not sure if you qualify? The means test calculator can help estimate your eligibility.

What If You Do Not Pass?

File Chapter 13 instead - No means test required (but note the 79.6% dismissal rate in W.D. Tenn.)

Wait for income to drop - The test uses the last 6 months' income

Special circumstances - Military service, serious illness, or job loss may qualify for exemptions

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

Free Discharge Screener

Open Bankruptcy Project Network

OBP Home
1328(f) Screener
Ch. 7 vs 13

## Eliminating Medical Debt in Memphis Bankruptcy - Tennessee
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/memphis/medical-debt.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsFAQMemphisNashvilleKnoxvilleChattanoogaOBP

Home›Memphis›Medical Debt

Eliminating Medical Debt in Memphis Bankruptcy

Medical debt is the leading cause of bankruptcy in America. In Memphis, medical bills are fully dischargeable in both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13.

Medical Debt in Bankruptcy

Medical debt is 100% dischargeable in bankruptcy. It is treated as general unsecured debt with no special protections for creditors.

Whether you owe $5,000 or $500,000 in medical bills, bankruptcy can eliminate it entirely. In Chapter 7, medical debt is wiped out in 3-4 months. In Chapter 13, you may pay a portion through your plan, with the remainder discharged.

Before Filing: Know Your Options

Negotiate directly: Many Memphis hospitals offer charity care or payment reductions

Check for billing errors: Medical billing errors are common

Statute of limitations: Tennessee's statute of limitations on medical debt is 6 years

Credit reporting: Medical debt under $500 no longer appears on credit reports

If negotiation is not enough and you have other debts too, bankruptcy may be the most effective solution.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

Free Discharge Screener

Open Bankruptcy Project Network

OBP Home
1328(f) Screener
Ch. 7 vs 13
Automatic Stay

Means Test

## Bankruptcy in Arlington, TN - Tennessee
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/memphis/suburbs/arlington.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsFAQMemphisNashvilleKnoxvilleChattanoogaOBP

Home›Memphis›Arlington

Bankruptcy in Arlington, TN

Arlington, TN residents considering bankruptcy can find help here. As part of the Memphis metro area, Arlington filers are served by the W.D. Tenn..

13,265

Population (est.)

$5,000 / $7,500

Homestead Exemption

$10,000 wildcard

Vehicle Protection

$338

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

Where to File

W.D. Tenn.

200 Jefferson Ave, Suite 413, Memphis, TN 38103

Tennessee Exemptions

Tennessee allows you to choose between state and federal exemptions.

PropertyTennessee ExemptionFederal Alternative

Homestead$5,000 individual / $7,500 family$27,900

Vehicle$10,000 (wildcard)$4,450

Household goods$4,000 total$700 per item / $14,875 total

Wildcard$10,000$1,475 + up to $13,950 unused homestead

Retirement (IRA/401k)Fully exemptFully exempt

Social SecurityFully exemptFully exempt

Full exemption details →

Bankruptcy Options

Chapter 7

Eliminates most unsecured debt in 3-4 months. Filing fee: $338. Attorney fees: $900-$1,800.
Chapter 7 guide →

Chapter 13

Keep property, repay over 3-5 years. Filing fee: $313. Attorney fees: $2,500-$4,000.
Chapter 13 guide →

Frequently Asked Questions

Where do Arlington, TN residents file for bankruptcy?

W.D. Tenn. at 200 Jefferson Ave, Suite 413, Memphis, TN 38103.

How much does bankruptcy cost in Arlington, TN?

Chapter 7: $1,238-$2,188 total. Chapter 13: $2,838-$4,363. Full breakdown.

What exemptions apply?

Tennessee state or federal exemptions - your choice. State homestead: $5,000/$7,500. Federal homestead: $27,900. Full list.

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

## Bankruptcy in Olive Branch, MS - Tennessee
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/memphis/suburbs/olive-branch-ms.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsFAQMemphisNashvilleKnoxvilleChattanoogaOBP

Home›Memphis›Olive Branch

Bankruptcy in Olive Branch, MS

Olive Branch, MS residents considering bankruptcy can find help here. As part of the Memphis metro area, Olive Branch filers are served by the Northern District of Mississippi.

42,305

Population (est.)

$75,000

Homestead Exemption

$10,000

Vehicle Protection

$338

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

Where to File

Northern District of Mississippi

703 N. 1st St, Oxford, MS 38655 (Aberdeen Division also serves DeSoto County)

Mississippi Jurisdiction Notice

Olive Branch, MS is in Mississippi, not Tennessee. Residents file in the Northern District of Mississippi, not the Western District of Tennessee. Mississippi has different exemptions and procedures. We include Olive Branch, MS in the Memphis metro guide because it is part of the greater Memphis area, but Mississippi law governs your filing.

Mississippi Exemptions

PropertyMississippi Exemption

Homestead$75,000 (160 acres)

Vehicle$10,000

Personal property$10,000

RetirementFully exempt

Mississippi does NOT allow federal exemptions. State exemptions only.

Bankruptcy Options

Chapter 7

Eliminates most unsecured debt in 3-4 months. Filing fee: $338. Attorney fees: $900-$1,800.
Chapter 7 guide →

Chapter 13

Keep property, repay over 3-5 years. Filing fee: $313. Attorney fees: $2,500-$4,000.
Chapter 13 guide →

Frequently Asked Questions

Where do Olive Branch, MS residents file for bankruptcy?

Northern District of Mississippi at 703 N. 1st St, Oxford, MS 38655 (Aberdeen Division also serves DeSoto County).

How much does bankruptcy cost in Olive Branch, MS?

Chapter 7: $1,238-$2,188 total. Chapter 13: $2,838-$4,363. Full breakdown.

What exemptions apply?

Mississippi state exemptions only. Homestead: $75,000. Vehicle: $10,000. Full list.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

Free Discharge Screener

## Memphis Bankruptcy Timeline - How Long Does It Take? - Tennessee
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/memphis/timeline.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsFAQMemphisNashvilleKnoxvilleChattanoogaOBP

Home›Memphis›Timeline

Memphis Bankruptcy Timeline - How Long Does It Take?

From pre-filing preparation to discharge, here is a detailed timeline for filing bankruptcy in Memphis.

Chapter 7 Timeline

1Before filing: Complete credit counseling course (up to 180 days before)

2Day 1: File petition with W.D. Tenn. Automatic stay takes effect immediately.

3Day 20-40: 341 meeting of creditors

4Day 30-60: Complete financial management course

5Day 60-90: Deadline for creditor objections

6Day 90-120: Discharge granted (3-4 months total)

7After discharge: Case closed. Begin rebuilding credit.

Chapter 13 Timeline

1Before filing: Complete credit counseling course

2Day 1: File petition and proposed plan. Automatic stay takes effect.

3Day 30: Begin plan payments (before confirmation)

4Day 20-50: 341 meeting of creditors

5Day 45-120: Confirmation hearing

6Years 1-5: Make monthly plan payments

7End of plan: Complete financial management course, receive discharge

Remember: 79.6% of Chapter 13 cases in W.D. Tenn. end in dismissal before reaching discharge.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

Free Discharge Screener

Open Bankruptcy Project Network

OBP Home
1328(f) Screener
Ch. 7 vs 13
Automatic Stay
341 Meeting
Means Test
Cost Guide
Dismissed?
File Again?
How to File

Free, open-source bankruptcy transparency. No ads. No affiliate links. Supported by donations.

An Open Bankruptcy Project resource

Data sourced from the Federal Judicial Center Integrated Database (2008--2024).

This site provides general information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for your specific situation.

© 2026 Open Bankruptcy Project. GitHub

## The 341 Meeting in Nashville - What to Expect - Tennessee
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/nashville/341-meeting.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsFAQMemphisNashvilleKnoxvilleChattanoogaOBP

Home›Nashville›341 Meeting

The 341 Meeting in Nashville - What to Expect

The 341 meeting of creditors is a brief hearing about 30 days after filing. Here is what Nashville filers need to know.

What Is the 341 Meeting?

The 341 meeting of creditors is a required hearing where you answer questions under oath. Conducted by the trustee, not a judge. Typically lasts 5-10 minutes.

Where and When

Location: Near the M.D. Tenn. courthouse at 701 Broadway, Nashville, TN 37203.

Timing: 20-40 days after filing.

What to Bring

Government-issued photo ID

Social Security card or proof of SSN

Recent pay stubs (last 60 days)

Most recent tax return

Bank statements (last 2-3 months)

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

Free Discharge Screener

Open Bankruptcy Project Network

OBP Home
1328(f) Screener
Ch. 7 vs 13
Automatic Stay
341 Meeting
Means Test
Cost Guide
Dismissed?
File Again?
How to File

## Chapter 13 Bankruptcy in Nashville - Tennessee
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/nashville/chapter-13.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsFAQMemphisNashvilleKnoxvilleChattanoogaOBP

Home›Nashville›Chapter 13

Chapter 13 Bankruptcy in Nashville

Chapter 13 lets you keep your property while repaying debts over 3-5 years. Here is what Nashville filers need to know.

How Chapter 13 Works

You propose a repayment plan paying back some or all debts over 3-5 years. Monthly payments go to a trustee. Useful for saving homes from foreclosure and catching up on car payments.

Save your home - catch up on mortgage arrears

Keep your car - cure defaults

Stop garnishments - automatic stay halts collections

Chapter 13 Timeline

1Credit counseling + file petition

2Begin payments within 30 days

3341 meeting (20-50 days after filing)

4Confirmation hearing

53-5 years of plan payments

6Discharge

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

Free Discharge Screener

Open Bankruptcy Project Network

OBP Home
1328(f) Screener
Ch. 7 vs 13
Automatic Stay
341 Meeting
Means Test
Cost Guide
Dismissed?
File Again?
How to File

Free, open-source bankruptcy transparency. No ads. No affiliate links. Supported by donations.

An Open Bankruptcy Project resource

## Chapter 7 Bankruptcy in Nashville - Tennessee
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/nashville/chapter-7.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsFAQMemphisNashvilleKnoxvilleChattanoogaOBP

Home›Nashville›Chapter 7

Chapter 7 Bankruptcy in Nashville

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts in 3-4 months. Here is what Nashville residents need to know.

What Is Chapter 7?

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts (credit cards, medical bills, personal loans). A trustee reviews your assets, but most Nashville cases are "no-asset" - you keep everything within exemption limits.

Debts Chapter 7 Eliminates

Dischargeable

Credit card debt

Medical bills

Personal loans

Utility bills

Some older tax debts

NOT Dischargeable

Student loans (usually)

Recent taxes

Child support / alimony

Debts from fraud

Criminal fines

Nashville Means Test

The means test compares your income to Tennessee's median. Full means test guide.

HouseholdAnnual Median

1 person$53,697

2 persons$67,854

3 persons$77,379

4 persons$94,416

Chapter 7 Timeline

1Credit counseling

2File petition at M.D. Tenn., 701 Broadway, Nashville, TN 37203

3341 meeting - ~30 days after filing

4Financial management course

5Discharge - 3-4 months total

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

Free Discharge Screener

Open Bankruptcy Project Network

OBP Home
1328(f) Screener
Ch. 7 vs 13
Automatic Stay
341 Meeting
Means Test
Cost Guide
Dismissed?
File Again?

## How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Nashville? - Tennessee
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/nashville/cost.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsFAQMemphisNashvilleKnoxvilleChattanoogaOBP

Home›Nashville›Cost

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Nashville?

A complete breakdown of bankruptcy costs for Nashville residents.

Cost Breakdown

ExpenseChapter 7Chapter 13

Court filing fee$338$313

Attorney fees (typical)$1,000 - $2,000$2,500 - $4,500

Credit counseling$25 - $50$25 - $50

Total$1,363 - $2,388$2,838 - $4,863

Ways to Reduce Costs

Fee waiver (Ch. 7): Income below 150% of poverty guidelines

Installment payments: Pay filing fee over time

Ch. 13 fees through plan: Attorney fees paid through repayment plan

Legal aid: Legal Aid Society of Middle Tennessee at (615) 244-6610

This site provides general educational information about bankruptcy law, not legal advice. Every situation is different. Consult with a qualified bankruptcy attorney licensed in Tennessee before making any decisions about filing.

## Nashville Bankruptcy Exemptions - State vs Federal - Tennessee
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/nashville/exemptions.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsFAQMemphisNashvilleKnoxvilleChattanoogaOBP

Home›Nashville›Exemptions

Nashville Bankruptcy Exemptions - State vs Federal

Tennessee lets you choose between state and federal exemptions. Compare both to protect the most property.

State vs. Federal Exemptions

Tennessee allows filers to choose between state and federal exemptions. You must pick one system - no mixing.

PropertyTennessee ExemptionFederal Alternative

Homestead$5,000 individual / $7,500 family$27,900

Vehicle$10,000 (wildcard)$4,450

Household goods$4,000 total$700 per item / $14,875 total

Wildcard$10,000$1,475 + up to $13,950 unused homestead

Retirement (IRA/401k)Fully exemptFully exempt

Social SecurityFully exemptFully exempt

For most Nashville homeowners, federal exemptions protect significantly more home equity ($27,900 vs $5,000/$7,500).

## Nashville Bankruptcy FAQ - Common Questions Answered - Tennessee
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/nashville/faq.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsFAQMemphisNashvilleKnoxvilleChattanoogaOBP

Home›Nashville›FAQ

Nashville Bankruptcy FAQ - Common Questions Answered

Answers to common questions about filing bankruptcy in Nashville, Tennessee.

How much does bankruptcy cost in Nashville?

Chapter 7: $1,363-$2,388. Chapter 13: $2,838-$4,863. Full breakdown.

Where do I file in Nashville?

Middle District of Tennessee, 701 Broadway, Nashville, TN 37203. Phone: (615) 736-5584.

Will I lose my house?

Usually not. Federal homestead exemption is $27,900. Learn more.

Will I lose my car?

Usually not. Tennessee's $10,000 wildcard covers most vehicles. Learn more.

What is the means test?

Compares your income to Tennessee's median. Below = qualify for Chapter 7. Details.

How long does bankruptcy take?

Chapter 7: 3-4 months. Chapter 13: 3-5 years.

What is the automatic stay?

Court order stopping foreclosure, repossession, garnishment, and collection actions. Learn more.

Can I file again?

Yes, with timing limits. Ch 7 to Ch 7: 8 years. Ch 13 to Ch 13: 2 years. Check eligibility.

How long does bankruptcy stay on my credit?

Chapter 7: 10 years. Chapter 13: 7 years.

Should I file Chapter 7 or Chapter 13?

Depends on income, assets, and goals. Compare chapters.

Bankruptcy in Nashville, Tennessee

Nashville residents file in the Middle District of Tennessee. This free guide covers everything Music City residents need to know about filing bankruptcy, from exemptions to costs to what happens after.

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13: Which Is Right for You?

Chapter 7 - Fresh Start

Eliminates most unsecured debts in 3-4 months. Must pass the means test.

Discharge in 3-4 months

Eliminates credit cards, medical debt

Filing fee: $338

Chapter 13 - Payment Plan

Keep your property while repaying debts over 3-5 years.

3-5 year repayment plan

Keep your home and car

Filing fee: $313

Tennessee uses non-judicial foreclosure, making the automatic stay critical for Nashville homeowners facing foreclosure.

## Can You Keep Your Car in Nashville Bankruptcy? - Tennessee
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/nashville/keep-your-car.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsFAQMemphisNashvilleKnoxvilleChattanoogaOBP

Home›Nashville›Keep Your Car

Can You Keep Your Car in Nashville Bankruptcy?

Most Nashville residents can keep their car in bankruptcy. Here is how.

Vehicle Exemptions

SystemVehicle Protection

Tennessee state$10,000 wildcard

Federal$4,450 vehicle + $1,475 wildcard + up to $13,950 unused homestead

Options for Cars with Loans

Reaffirmation: Keep paying, keep the car

Redemption: Pay current value in lump sum

Surrender: Return car, discharge remaining debt

Chapter 13 cramdown: Reduce balance to current value (loan must be 910+ days old)

Keeping Your Car in a Nashville Bankruptcy

For Nashville filers, the car is often the second most important asset to protect after the house. Your car is how you get to work, how you get to the bankruptcy court, and - if you lose it - often how you spiral back into financial trouble. The good news: most filers keep their vehicles. The Bankruptcy Code offers three separate tools (reaffirmation, redemption, and cramdown) and the state motor vehicle exemption protects the equity in paid-off cars. Here is how to use each one.

Reaffirmation (Chapter 7). If you are current on your car loan and want to keep paying, you sign a reaffirmation agreement with the lender. Reaffirmation removes the car loan from the discharge, meaning you remain personally liable going forward. The judge has to approve reaffirmation for represented debtors and must find that it does not impose an undue hardship. Most lenders require reaffirmation to continue reporting on-time payments to credit bureaus, so reaffirming can help rebuild credit - but if you default later, the lender can sue you for a deficiency after repossession. Think carefully before reaffirming on a car you are upside-down on.

Redemption (Chapter 7). Under 11 U.S.C. section 722, you can redeem a car by paying the lender the car's current fair market value in a single lump sum. If you owe $15,000 on a car worth $8,000, you can wipe out the lien by paying the lender $8,000. The catch is finding the cash - many filers use a redemption lender like 722 Redemption Funding. Redemption is powerful for underwater cars but not available if you are current and want to stay that way.

Cramdown (Chapter 13). If you bought the car more than 910 days before filing and it is worth less than you owe, Chapter 13 lets you "cram down" the loan to the car's current value. The secured portion is paid at the car's value over the plan term with a new interest rate set by the court (usually prime plus a risk premium). The deficiency - the difference between the original loan balance and the new secured amount - is treated as unsecured and usually paid pennies on the dollar. The 910-day rule cuts off cramdown for newer purchase-money car loans, but any refinance, any non-purchase-money lien, or any vehicle purchased more than 910 days ago is fair game.

Paid-Off Cars and the Exemption

If your car is paid off, the question is whether your equity fits within the Tennessee motor vehicle exemption (and any unused wildcard exemption you can stack on top). State exemption amounts typically run from $2,000 to $10,000 per vehicle. If the car is worth more than your exemption, a Chapter 7 trustee could theoretically sell it - but in practice, trustees rarely bother unless the excess equity is substantial. In Chapter 13, you keep the car and pay the non-exempt value to unsecured creditors through the plan.

Frequently Asked Questions

What happens to my car payment during the bankruptcy case?

You keep paying it. The automatic stay stops repossession, but it does not excuse you from making your monthly payment. If you want to keep the car, stay current. Some trustees allow you to skip one payment at filing if you are rolling it into a Chapter 13 plan, but the default assumption is you keep paying the car lender directly.

Can I keep my car in a Nashville Chapter 7 if I am behind on payments?

Generally no, unless you can catch up before the reaffirmation deadline. Chapter 7 does not have a cure mechanism for car loan arrears the way it does for mortgages. If you are behind, Chapter 13 is the answer - Chapter 13 lets you add the arrearage to your plan payment and cure the default over 36 to 60 months while keeping the car.

Is cramdown available on a car I bought last year?

No. The 'hanging paragraph' at the end of 11 U.S.C. section 1325(a) bars cramdown for purchase-money security interests in vehicles acquired within 910 days before filing, where the vehicle is for personal use. Non-purchase-money loans (refinances, private lenders) and vehicles purchased for business use or owned longer than 910 days remain eligible for cramdown.

Related resources from the Bankruptcy Transparency Network: means test, Chapter 7 vs. 13, 1328(f) prior-discharge screener, 341 meeting, automatic stay, how to file bankruptcy, student loans, Open Bankruptcy Project.

## Can You Keep Your House in Nashville Bankruptcy? - Tennessee
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/nashville/keep-your-house.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsFAQMemphisNashvilleKnoxvilleChattanoogaOBP

Home›Nashville›Keep Your House

Can You Keep Your House in Nashville Bankruptcy?

Tennessee has a low state homestead exemption, but you can choose federal exemptions for much better protection.

Homestead Exemption Comparison

SystemProtection

TN state (individual)$5,000

TN state (family/62+)$7,500

Federal$27,900

Federal exemptions protect over 5x more equity. Most Nashville homeowners should choose federal.

Non-Judicial Foreclosure

Tennessee is a non-judicial foreclosure state. The automatic stay is your strongest defense against foreclosure.

Keeping Your House in a Nashville Bankruptcy

For most Nashville homeowners, keeping the house is the top priority in any bankruptcy filing. The good news: bankruptcy was not designed to take your home. Between the homestead exemption and the Chapter 13 cure provisions, the great majority of filers keep their houses and come out the other side with their mortgage current and their unsecured debts discharged. Here is how it works.

The first question is whether you are current on your mortgage. If you are, and your equity is within the Tennessee homestead exemption, you can file Chapter 7 and simply keep making your mortgage payments throughout the case. The mortgage lender cannot accelerate the loan just because you filed - ipso facto clauses are unenforceable under 11 U.S.C. section 365(e). This is called "riding through": you keep paying, the lender keeps accepting, and the lien survives the discharge.

If you are behind on the mortgage and the lender has started or is about to start foreclosure, Chapter 13 is almost always the better choice. Chapter 13 lets you "cure" a mortgage arrearage over the full length of your plan - up to 60 months - while keeping your regular monthly payment current. So if you owe $18,000 in back payments and you have a 60-month plan, you add $300 per month to your Chapter 13 payment to catch up the arrearage, and the bank cannot foreclose as long as you stay current on both the arrearage and the ongoing mortgage. This is one of Chapter 13's superpowers.

Equity and the Homestead Exemption

If your home equity exceeds your Tennessee homestead exemption, a Chapter 7 trustee could theoretically sell the house, pay you the exempt portion, and distribute the rest to unsecured creditors. In practice, trustees rarely actually sell houses - the transaction costs (commissions, carrying costs, title issues) often eat up any recovery. But if your non-exempt equity is substantial, the trustee may demand a buy-back payment or push you into Chapter 13. In Chapter 13, non-exempt equity gets paid to unsecureds through the plan over three to five years, and you keep the house.

Second Mortgages and Lien Stripping

If you have a second mortgage or HELOC and your first mortgage balance is higher than the home's current market value, you may be able to "strip" the second lien in Chapter 13. A wholly unsecured junior lien can be voided and treated as unsecured debt, which usually means paying pennies on the dollar through the plan. This does not work in Chapter 7 - Dewsnup v. Timm (1992) forbids lien stripping in Chapter 7 - and it does not work if the second is even one dollar in the money.

Frequently Asked Questions

Will filing bankruptcy in Nashville trigger foreclosure?

No. The opposite. Filing triggers the automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. section 362, which immediately halts any pending foreclosure sale, stops lender calls, and freezes all collection activity. If a foreclosure sale is scheduled for tomorrow, filing today stops it. The lender has to get relief from the stay before proceeding, which takes weeks or months and gives you time to cure the arrearage through Chapter 13.

Can I discharge my mortgage in a Nashville Chapter 7?

You can discharge your personal liability on the mortgage note, but the lien on the house survives. That means if you stop paying after discharge, the lender can foreclose on the collateral but cannot sue you for a deficiency. This is sometimes useful for underwater houses you plan to surrender - no risk of a deficiency lawsuit post-discharge.

How much home equity can I protect in Tennessee?

The Tennessee homestead exemption amount varies by state and is updated periodically. Some states allow only a few thousand dollars, others allow hundreds of thousands, and a handful (Florida, Texas, Kansas, Iowa, Oklahoma, South Dakota) allow unlimited equity subject to acreage caps. Always check the current Tennessee statute before assuming you are covered.

Related resources from the Bankruptcy Transparency Network: means test, Chapter 7 vs. 13, 1328(f) prior-discharge screener, 341 meeting, automatic stay, how to file bankruptcy, student loans, Open Bankruptcy Project.

## Nashville Means Test - Do You Qualify for Chapter 7? - Tennessee
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/nashville/means-test.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsFAQMemphisNashvilleKnoxvilleChattanoogaOBP

Home›Nashville›Means Test

Nashville Means Test - Do You Qualify for Chapter 7?

The means test determines Chapter 7 eligibility by comparing your income to Tennessee's state median.

2026 Tennessee Median Income

HouseholdAnnualMonthly

1 person$53,697$4,475

2 persons$67,854$5,655

3 persons$77,379$6,448

4 persons$94,416$7,868

Add $9,900 for each person beyond 4. Full details at meanstest.org.

Walking Through the Nashville Means Test Step by Step

The bankruptcy means test is the federal gatekeeper between you and Chapter 7 relief. For Nashville residents, it has two stages, and most filers pass at the first stage without ever needing the second. The test compares your last six months of gross household income (multiplied by two for an annualized figure) against the Tennessee state median shown above. If you are under the median, you automatically qualify for Chapter 7 and the rest of the test does not apply.

If your annualized income is above the Tennessee median, the means test moves to a second calculation. You subtract a set of allowed monthly expenses - IRS National Standards for food, clothing, and household supplies; Local Standards for housing and transportation set specifically for Davidson County; your actual health insurance, childcare, and court-ordered support payments; and secured debt payments on your house and car. What is left is your "monthly disposable income." If that number is low enough, you still pass.

A few Nashville-specific notes: the Middle District of Tennessee uses the IRS Local Standards for Davidson and surrounding counties, which tend to be higher than rural Tennessee counties. That can help borderline filers. Social Security retirement and disability income is excluded entirely from the income calculation. VA disability is also excluded. If you are a disabled veteran whose Chapter 7 debts are primarily from active duty, you can skip the means test altogether under 11 U.S.C. section 707(b)(2)(D).

Common Mistakes to Avoid

Counting only your income. The test looks at household income, including a non-filing spouse, with certain marital deductions available.

Using gross pay from a pay stub. Use actual deposits over the six months preceding the month you file. Overtime, bonuses, and commissions all count.

Forgetting tax refunds. Refunds are not "income" for the means test, but unspent refunds sitting in your account on the filing date are assets the trustee can reach.

Filing the wrong month. A single high-income month rolling off your six-month window can drop you below median. Timing matters.

If the means test is the only thing standing between you and discharge, a Nashville Chapter 13 case is almost always available as a fallback - Chapter 13 has no means test at the front door, only a disposable income calculation for the plan payment.

Frequently Asked Questions

Does the Nashville means test count my spouse's income if I file alone?

Yes. Household income is combined for the means test regardless of who is filing. However, a non-filing spouse's income spent solely on their own separate obligations - their own car loan, their separate credit card, their student loans - can be deducted as a 'marital adjustment' on Form 122A-1 Line 13.

What if my income just went down but the six-month lookback still shows me above median?

You have two choices. First, wait to file until enough low-income months roll into the lookback window to drop you below median. Second, file now under Chapter 13 and convert to Chapter 7 later once your six-month average drops. A third option - arguing 'special circumstances' under section 707(b)(2)(B) - is available but requires documented unique circumstances like a serious medical condition or active-duty call-up.

Are VA disability payments counted in the Nashville means test?

No. VA disability, Social Security retirement, and Social Security disability (SSDI) are all excluded from current monthly income for means test purposes. If you are a disabled veteran whose debts are primarily incurred during active duty or while performing a homeland defense activity, you may be fully exempt from the means test under 11 U.S.C. section 707(b)(2)(D).

Related resources from the Bankruptcy Transparency Network: means test, Chapter 7 vs. 13, 1328(f) prior-discharge screener, 341 meeting, automatic stay, how to file bankruptcy, student loans, Open Bankruptcy Project.

## Bankruptcy in Franklin, TN - Tennessee

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsFAQMemphisNashvilleKnoxvilleChattanoogaOBP

Home›Nashville›Franklin

Bankruptcy in Franklin, TN

Franklin, TN residents considering bankruptcy can find help here. As part of the Nashville metro area, Franklin filers are served by the M.D. Tenn..

87,521

Population (est.)

$5,000 / $7,500

Homestead Exemption

$10,000 wildcard

Vehicle Protection

$338

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

Where to File

M.D. Tenn.

701 Broadway, Nashville, TN 37203

Tennessee Exemptions

Tennessee allows you to choose between state and federal exemptions.

PropertyTennessee ExemptionFederal Alternative

Homestead$5,000 individual / $7,500 family$27,900

Vehicle$10,000 (wildcard)$4,450

Bankruptcy Options

Chapter 7

Eliminates most unsecured debt in 3-4 months. Filing fee: $338. Attorney fees: $900-$1,800.
Chapter 7 guide →

Chapter 13

Keep property, repay over 3-5 years. Filing fee: $313. Attorney fees: $2,500-$4,000.
Chapter 13 guide →

Frequently Asked Questions

Where do Franklin, TN residents file for bankruptcy?

M.D. Tenn. at 701 Broadway, Nashville, TN 37203.

How much does bankruptcy cost in Franklin, TN?

Chapter 7: $1,238-$2,188 total. Chapter 13: $2,838-$4,363. Full breakdown.

What exemptions apply?

Tennessee state or federal exemptions - your choice. State homestead: $5,000/$7,500. Federal homestead: $27,900. Full list.

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

## Payday Loans and Bankruptcy in Tennessee
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/payday-loans.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeMemphisNashvilleChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide›Payday Loans

Payday Loans and Bankruptcy in Tennessee

Payday loans trap borrowers in cycles of high-interest debt. Bankruptcy can break that cycle by discharging payday loan obligations entirely.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Payday Loans Are Dischargeable

Payday loans are unsecured consumer debt. They are fully dischargeable in bankruptcy - meaning you will not owe anything after your case is completed.

Chapter 7: Payday loan debt is eliminated entirely in 3-4 months

Chapter 13: Payday loans are included in your repayment plan as general unsecured debt, typically receiving only a fraction of the balance

Stopping ACH Debits and Post-Dated Checks

Many payday lenders require ACH (electronic) authorization or post-dated checks as a condition of the loan. Once you file bankruptcy:

The automatic stay prohibits the lender from processing any further debits

Revoke your ACH authorization in writing with both the lender and your bank

Notify your bank of the bankruptcy filing and request a stop payment on any post-dated checks

If the lender processes a payment after filing, they have violated the stay and may owe you damages

Take action immediately upon filing: contact your bank, revoke ACH authorizations in writing, and keep copies of all communications.

Can Payday Lenders Challenge Discharge?

In rare cases, a payday lender might argue that the loan was obtained through fraud (11 U.S.C. section 523(a)(2)). To succeed, they would need to prove:

You made a false representation (e.g., lied about income or employment)

You intended to deceive the lender

The lender relied on your misrepresentation

In practice, payday lenders rarely challenge discharge because the amounts are small and the legal costs are high. Taking out a payday loan when you know you plan to file bankruptcy could raise issues, so consult an attorney about timing.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I discharge payday loans in bankruptcy in Tennessee?

Yes. Payday loans are unsecured debt and are fully dischargeable in both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 bankruptcy in Tennessee. Once discharged, the lender cannot collect, even if they have post-dated checks or ACH authorization.

Can a payday lender cash my check after I file bankruptcy?

No. The automatic stay prevents payday lenders from cashing post-dated checks or processing ACH debits after you file. If they do, it is a stay violation and you may be entitled to damages.

Should I stop paying payday loans before filing?

Once you decide to file bankruptcy, continuing to pay payday loans means less money available for necessary living expenses. However, consult an attorney about timing, especially regarding any pending ACH authorizations or post-dated checks.

Break the Payday Loan Cycle

Check if you qualify for a bankruptcy discharge to eliminate payday loan debt.

Discharge Screener
Tennessee Chapter 7 Guide

## Undoing a Reaffirmation Agreement in Tennessee - 60-Day Rescission, Options
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/reaffirmation-regret.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostGlossaryFaqOBP

Home›Undoing a Reaffirmation Agreement in Tennessee

Undoing a Reaffirmation Agreement in Tennessee

Reaffirmation agreements make you personally liable for a debt that would otherwise be discharged. If you regret signing one, you may have options - especially if you are still within the 60-day rescission window.

Quick answer: You can rescind (cancel) a reaffirmation agreement within 60 days of filing it with the court, or before discharge, whichever is later (11 U.S.C. section 524(c)(4)). No reason needed - just send written notice to the creditor. After 60 days, options are limited but include negotiation, voluntary surrender, or a new filing.

The 60-Day Rescission Window

Under 11 U.S.C. section 524(c)(4), you have an absolute right to rescind any reaffirmation agreement at any time before discharge or within 60 days after the agreement is filed with the court, whichever is later.

No reason required - you do not have to explain why

Written notice to the creditor is sufficient

The debt becomes dischargeable again once rescinded

The creditor may repossess the collateral (vehicle, etc.) after rescission

If you are considering rescission, act quickly. Count the days carefully from when the agreement was filed with the court (check your case docket), not when you signed it.

Options After the 60-Day Window

If You Cannot Afford the Payments

After the rescission window closes, you have several options:

Negotiate with the creditor: Request lower payments, reduced interest, or loan modification. Creditors often prefer this to repossession/foreclosure.

Refinance: If your credit has improved since filing, refinancing at a lower rate may make payments affordable.

Voluntary surrender: Return the property. You will owe any deficiency balance (the difference between what you owe and what the property sells for). This is a regular debt - not discharged because you reaffirmed it.

File a new bankruptcy: If eligible under 11 U.S.C. section 727(a)(8) (Chapter 7: 8 years between filings) or section 1328(f) (Chapter 13: varies), the reaffirmed debt can be discharged in the new case.

Tennessee-Specific Considerations

Tennessee uses state exemptions only. Tennessee's motor vehicle exemption is quite limited. The E.D. Tenn., M.D. Tenn., and W.D. Tenn. courts all review reaffirmation agreements. The 60-day rescission period applies. Tennessee Consumer Protection Act (Tenn. Code 47-18) provides additional remedies against deceptive practices.

Common Reaffirmation Regrets

Vehicle Reaffirmation

The most common reaffirmation regret involves vehicles. Debtors reaffirm a car loan to keep the vehicle but later realize the payment is unsustainable or the car is worth far less than the loan balance. Without reaffirmation, the debt would have been discharged - you could have surrendered the car and owed nothing.

Mortgage Reaffirmation

Most attorneys advise against mortgage reaffirmation. Without reaffirmation, you can keep making payments and stay in the home. If you ever need to walk away, the deficiency is discharged. With reaffirmation, you are personally liable for any deficiency after foreclosure.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I undo a reaffirmation agreement?

Within 60 days of filing or before discharge: yes, automatically. After that: options are limited to negotiation, refinancing, surrender, or a new bankruptcy filing.

What happens after the 60-day window?

The agreement is binding. You can negotiate with the creditor, refinance, voluntarily surrender, or file a new bankruptcy if eligible.

Do I need a reason to rescind?

No. The right to rescind within 60 days is absolute - no reason or justification needed. Simply send written notice to the creditor.

What if the court approved my reaffirmation?

Court approval does not prevent rescission during the 60-day window. The right to rescind under section 524(c)(4) applies regardless of court approval.

Should I have reaffirmed in the first place?

That depends on the specific asset and your financial situation. Many attorneys advise against reaffirmation (especially on mortgages) because it restores personal liability on debt that could have been discharged.

Related Topics

Exemptions
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House
Credit After
Wage Garnishment
Medical Debt

## Renting After Bankruptcy in Tennessee - Landlord Screening, Tips, Tenant Rights
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/renting-after-bankruptcy.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostGlossaryFaqOBP

Home›Renting After Bankruptcy in Tennessee

Renting After Bankruptcy in Tennessee

Bankruptcy does not prevent you from renting. Many landlords will approve applicants with a bankruptcy on their record if you can show stable income and a reliable rental history. Here is how to navigate the process in Tennessee.

Quick answer: You can rent after bankruptcy. Landlords can see the filing on your credit report, but it is not an automatic disqualifier. Focus on stable income, rental references, an honest explanation, and (if needed) a larger security deposit. Government housing assistance cannot be denied based on bankruptcy under section 525(a).

What Landlords See on Your Credit Report

When a landlord runs a credit check, they will see:

The bankruptcy filing - Chapter 7 (10 years) or Chapter 13 (7 years)

Discharged debts - accounts included in the bankruptcy will show as "included in bankruptcy" or "discharged"

Your credit score - typically drops significantly at filing but recovers over time

Post-filing payment history - new accounts opened after filing show your current reliability

Many landlords focus more on recent history than old entries. A bankruptcy from 2-3 years ago with perfect payment history since then is viewed very differently than a recent filing.

Tennessee Tenant Rights and Protections

Tennessee has the Uniform Residential Landlord and Tenant Act (Tenn. Code Ch. 66-28). Landlords can run credit checks and consider bankruptcy history. Tennessee does not have rent control. Nashville, Memphis, Knoxville, and Chattanooga metro areas have competitive rental markets with rising rents. Tennessee law does not prohibit denial based on bankruptcy. Providing rental references and proof of income is important.

Tips for Getting Approved

1. Prepare a Rental Resume

Include your rental history, employment details, income verification, and references from previous landlords. A well-organized application signals reliability.

2. Write a Brief Explanation Letter

Address the bankruptcy directly. Explain the circumstances (job loss, medical emergency, divorce) and emphasize what has changed. Show that you have stable income and have been managing finances responsibly since filing.

3. Offer a Larger Security Deposit

Offering an additional month's deposit reduces the landlord's risk and shows good faith. Check Tennessee law for any limits on security deposit amounts.

4. Consider Individual Landlords Over Large Complexes

Individual landlords and small property owners often have more flexibility in their screening criteria than large corporate-managed apartment complexes that use automated approval algorithms.

5. Get References

A letter from a previous landlord confirming on-time payments and good tenancy can outweigh a credit blemish. Employment references showing stable income also help.

Government Housing After Bankruptcy

Under 11 U.S.C. section 525(a), government agencies cannot deny public benefits based solely on a bankruptcy filing. This includes:

Section 8 Housing Choice Vouchers

Public housing

State housing assistance programs

FHA-insured mortgages (after applicable waiting periods)

The waiting period for FHA loans is 2 years after Chapter 7 discharge or 1 year into a Chapter 13 plan with court approval.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I rent after bankruptcy in Tennessee?

Yes. Many landlords will approve applicants with bankruptcy if you show stable income, good rental history, and an honest explanation of your filing.

How long does bankruptcy show on my credit report?

Chapter 7: 10 years. Chapter 13: 7 years. The impact on your score diminishes over time, especially with good post-filing payment history.

Can a landlord deny me for bankruptcy?

For new applications, landlords can consider bankruptcy as part of screening in most states. For renewals, some courts extend section 525 protections. Government housing cannot be denied based on bankruptcy.

Should I explain the bankruptcy to a landlord?

Yes. A brief, honest explanation shows responsibility and maturity. Emphasize what changed and your current financial stability.

Can I get Section 8 after bankruptcy?

Yes. Under section 525(a), government agencies cannot deny public benefits based on bankruptcy. Section 8, public housing, and other government housing programs remain available.

Related Topics

Exemptions
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House

## Repossession and Credit Reports After Bankruptcy in Tennessee
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/repo-credit-report.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeMemphisNashvilleChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Repo & Credit Report

Repossession and Credit Reports After Bankruptcy in Tennessee

Bankruptcy does not erase the repo from your credit report - but it eliminates the debt. Here is what changes, what stays, and how to fix errors.

Quick answer: The repossession entry stays on your credit report for 7 years from the date of first delinquency. Bankruptcy discharges the remaining balance (deficiency), which should be reported as $0. If the creditor still reports a balance after discharge, dispute it under the FCRA.

What Changes After Discharge

What Bankruptcy Fixes

Deficiency balance discharged - you owe $0

Collection activity must stop permanently

Account status should update to "discharged"

No more calls, letters, or lawsuits about the balance

Creditor cannot report a balance owed

What Bankruptcy Does Not Fix

Repossession entry remains on credit report

Late payment history leading up to repo remains

7-year reporting clock does not reset

Bankruptcy filing itself appears on credit report

Credit score impact from both events

Disputing Credit Report Errors

After bankruptcy discharge, review your credit reports from all three bureaus (Equifax, Experian, TransUnion) for these common errors:

Balance still showing: Should be $0 after discharge. This is the most common error.

"Past due" status: Should show "discharged in bankruptcy" or "included in bankruptcy."

Active collection: The account should not show as in active collection after discharge.

Incorrect dates: The date of first delinquency determines when the entry falls off. Verify this date is correct.

1

Pull Reports

Get free reports from annualcreditreport.com (all three bureaus)

2

Identify Errors

Compare each repo account against your discharge order

3

Dispute

File disputes online with each bureau. Include your discharge order as evidence.

FCRA Rights After Bankruptcy

The Fair Credit Reporting Act (FCRA) gives you powerful tools to correct your credit report after bankruptcy:

Dispute right: You can dispute any inaccurate information. The bureau has 30 days to investigate and correct or remove the entry.

Creditor obligations: Creditors who report to credit bureaus must update accounts to reflect the discharge. Failure to do so violates the FCRA.

Damages: If a creditor willfully reports incorrect information after discharge, you may be entitled to statutory damages ($100-$1,000), actual damages, and attorney fees under the FCRA.

Discharge injunction: Reporting a balance owed on a discharged debt may also violate the bankruptcy discharge injunction (11 U.S.C. section 524), which can result in contempt sanctions.

Rebuilding Credit After Repo + Bankruptcy

Secured credit card: Start with a secured card ($200-$500 deposit). Use it for small purchases and pay in full monthly.

Credit-builder loan: Some Tennessee credit unions offer loans specifically designed to rebuild credit.

Authorized user: Ask a trusted person to add you as an authorized user on their account.

On-time payments: Every on-time payment builds your score. Payment history is the largest factor (35%).

Time: The repo and bankruptcy impact your score less as they age. Most people see significant score improvement within 2-3 years of discharge.

Frequently Asked Questions

Does bankruptcy remove a repossession from my credit report in Tennessee?

No. Bankruptcy does not remove the repossession entry from your credit report. The repo stays on your report for 7 years from the date of first delinquency. However, the bankruptcy discharge eliminates the deficiency balance - the amount you still owe after the repo sale.

What happens to the repo balance after bankruptcy?

In Chapter 7, the deficiency balance (what you owe minus what the vehicle sold for) is discharged. In Chapter 13, it is treated as unsecured debt and paid at the plan percentage, often cents on the dollar. Either way, the balance should be reported as $0 after discharge.

How should a repo appear on my credit report after discharge?

After bankruptcy discharge, the repossession account should show: balance $0, 'included in bankruptcy' or 'discharged in bankruptcy.' If the report still shows a balance owed, that is a reporting error you can dispute.

Can I dispute a repo on my credit report after bankruptcy?

Yes. Under the FCRA (Fair Credit Reporting Act), you can dispute inaccurate information. If the repo account shows a balance after discharge, shows as 'past due' rather than 'discharged,' or has incorrect dates, file disputes with all three bureaus.

How long does a repo stay on my credit report in Tennessee?

A repossession stays on your credit report for 7 years from the date of first delinquency, regardless of bankruptcy. The bankruptcy filing itself stays for 7 years (Chapter 13) or 10 years (Chapter 7). These are federal rules that apply uniformly across all states including Tennessee.

Can the repo lender collect after bankruptcy discharge?

No. The discharge injunction under 11 U.S.C. section 524 permanently prohibits the creditor from collecting the discharged debt. If they attempt collection (calls, letters, reporting a balance), you may have a claim for violation of the discharge injunction.

Related Topics

Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Exemptions
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House
Credit After
Garnishment
FAQ

## Tennessee Bankruptcy Resources - Courts, Legal Aid, Credit Counseling
Source: https://tennesseebankruptcy.org/resources.html

Tennessee Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQStatsResourcesOBP

Home›Resources

Tennessee Bankruptcy Resources

Courts, legal aid organizations, credit counseling agencies, and free educational tools to help you navigate the bankruptcy process in Tennessee.

Bankruptcy Courts

Western District of Tennessee

Address: 200 Jefferson Ave, Suite 413, Memphis, TN 38103

Phone: (901) 328-3500

Website: tnwb.uscourts.gov

Middle District of Tennessee

Address: 701 Broadway, Nashville, TN 37203

Phone: (615) 736-5584

Website: tnmb.uscourts.gov

Eastern District of Tennessee

Address: 800 Market St, Suite 330, Knoxville, TN 37902

Phone: (865) 545-4279

Website: tneb.uscourts.gov

Legal Aid Organizations

Legal Aid Society of Middle Tennessee

Free legal services for low-income residents of the Nashville area.

Phone: (615) 244-6610

Website: las.org

Memphis Area Legal Services

Free legal help for low-income Memphis residents.

Phone: (901) 523-8822

Website: malsi.org

Legal Aid of East Tennessee

Free legal services for eastern Tennessee.

Phone: (865) 637-0484

Website: laet.org

Credit Counseling and Debtor Education

Two courses are required for bankruptcy: a pre-filing credit counseling course and a post-filing debtor education course. You must use agencies approved by the U.S. Trustee Program.

Approved agencies: justice.gov - approved agencies

Cost: $15-25 per course (reduced fees available)

Format: Online, phone, or in-person

Time: About 60-90 minutes per course

U.S. Trustee Program

The U.S. Trustee Program oversees the administration of bankruptcy cases.

Region 8: (901) 544-3251

Website: justice.gov/ust

Frequently Asked Questions

Where is the bankruptcy court in Tennessee?

Tennessee has 3 federal bankruptcy courts. W.D. Tenn.: 200 Jefferson Ave, Suite 413, Memphis, TN 38103 ((901) 328-3500); M.D. Tenn.: 701 Broadway, Nashville, TN 37203 ((615) 736-5584); E.D. Tenn.: 800 Market St, Suite 330, Knoxville, TN 37902 ((865) 545-4279)

Is there free legal help for bankruptcy in Tennessee?

Yes. Legal Aid Society of Middle Tennessee ((615) 244-6610) provides free legal services. Memphis Area Legal Services ((901) 523-8822) provides free legal services. Legal Aid of East Tennessee ((865) 637-0484) provides free legal services.

Where can I take the required credit counseling course?

The U.S. Trustee Program maintains a list of approved credit counseling agencies at justice.gov/ust. Most offer courses online or by phone for $15-25.
